                        espionage and information warfare, targeting              targeted India's power sector infrastructure,
                        India’s critical sectors and public perception.           especially near Ladakh, amid the border
                                                                                  standoff.


4.1 India–China Border Disputes
               Sector                               India’s Stand                                  China’s Stand
Western Sector                        India follows the Johnson Line (drawn in          China follows the McDonald Line
Ladakh (Aksai Chin)                   the 1860s), which includes Aksai Chin as          (1890s) and controls Aksai Chin since
                                      part of India.                                    the 1962 war.
Middle Sector                         This area is largely peaceful. Both sides have    China has no major objections in this
(Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh)       shared maps and there is basic agreement.         sector.
Eastern Sector (Arunachal             India follows the McMahon Line (1914),            China rejects the McMahon Line and
Pradesh, Sikkim)                      which includes Arunachal as Indian territory.     claims all of Arunachal as South Tibet.


                                     Reasons Behind China's Aggression
Strategic Infrastructure &          y India’s rapid development of border infrastructure (like the Darbuk–Shyok–DBO
Territorial Assertion                 road, bridges over Pangong Tso) is seen by China as a threat to its tactical
                                      dominance.
                                    y The abrogation of Article 370 and creation of Ladakh UT signalled India's
                                      reassertion near Aksai Chin.
China–Pakistan                      y The deepening China-Pakistan partnership—including CPEC and military ties—
Strategic Nexus                       intensifies the two-front threat for India.
                                    y China backs Pakistan’s position on Kashmir and provides strategic infrastructure
                                      (Gwadar Port) to counterbalance India.
India’s Rising Global & Strategic   y India’s growing role in global forums like QUAD and its projection as a democratic
Profile                               counterweight to China’s authoritarian model have increased Chinese strategic
                                      unease, prompting efforts to constrain India’s regional ambitions.
Hydrological Leverage &             y China controls the headwaters of key rivers like the Yarlung Tsangpo
Resource Control                      (Brahmaputra).
                                    y Its unannounced dam projects raise concerns over water security and the potential
                                      use of hydrological dominance as a strategic tool.

                                     India’s Response to Border Disputes
Military Deterrence                 Completion of coordinated patrolling protocols in Depsang Plains and Demchok,
                                    ensuring ground-level transparency without thinning the extra troops.
Diplomatic Realism                  SCO Summit (Tianjin 2025): India’s attendance signalled a pragmatic thaw, but India
                                    blocked any joint statement that didn't explicitly mention territorial sovereignty.
Infrastructure                      Closing the connectivity gap via the Vibrant Villages Programme, Border Area
                                    Development Programme and all-weather strategic tunnels to ensure rapid mobilisation
                                    and demographic security.
Maritime Strategy                   Strengthening the Indo-Pacific through the QUAD and upgrading the Andaman &
                                    Nicobar Islands (ANC) to monitor Chinese research/spy vessels
Kazan Consensus                     India and China formalised the border pact, directing Special Representatives (SRs) to
(BRICS 2024)                        meet and oversee the complete resolution of the boundary question.

                                                Border agreements
Agreement on the Maintenance        The first formal agreement where both nations pledged to respect the Line of Actual
of Peace and Tranquility, 1993      Control (LAC) and resolve the boundary question through peaceful negotiations while
                                    reducing troop levels to a minimum along the border.
Agreement on the Confidence-        Established specific protocols to prevent military escalation, such as banning the use of
Building Measures in the            firearms and hazardous chemicals along the LAC within a specific area and prohibiting
Military Field, 1996                large-scale military exercises in close proximity to the border.
Protocol on Modalities for the      Established detailed operational procedures for ground commanders to manage
Implementation of Confidence        face-offs, including standardised flag meetings, communication channels, and the
Building Measures in the            requirement to exercise self-restraint if patrols come into contact along the LAC
Military Field, 2005
Agreement on Border Defence         Focuses on enhancing coordination between border troops through a redefined no-tail
Cooperation, 2013                   policy, establishing a hotline between military headquarters, and standardising the right
                                    to seek clarification when patrol face-offs occur to prevent localised incidents from
                                    escalating.
The October 2024 Patrol             This pact restored patrolling rights in Depsang Plains and Demchok to the pre-April
Agreement                           2020 status quo.
The 2025 Buffer Zone Review         An administrative understanding to periodically review the temporary Buffer Zones
                                    (Galwan, Gogra-Hotsprings, and Pangong Tso) created during the 2021–2023
                                    disengagement.
23rd Corps Commander Meet           Both sides agreed to use existing local mechanisms to prevent ground-level
                                    skirmishes.


4.2.Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) and India’s Response
                                            Belt and Road Initiative (BRI)
The Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) was launched by China in 2013 and is a global infrastructure and connectivity project
comprising the Silk Road Economic Belt (land-based) and the 21st Century Maritime Silk Road (sea-based). It spans over
100 countries, aiming to boost trade, investment, and China’s global influence, covering around 70% of the world’s
population and over half of global GDP.

                                          India’s Concerns Related to BRI
Sovereignty             The CPEC passes through Pakistan-occupied Kashmir (PoK), an integral part of India. Endorsing
Violation               BRI would imply tacit acceptance of this territorial violation.
Opaque                  BRI projects often involve non-transparent funding, non-competitive contracts, and limited local
Financing               participation, raising concerns about fiscal sustainability and governance accountability.
Debt-Trap               Countries like Sri Lanka (Hambantota Port) and Pakistan have become heavily indebted,
Diplomacy               resulting in loss of economic sovereignty and strategic assets, reinforcing fears of Chinese neo-
                        colonialism.
Strategic               BRI facilitates China’s military and strategic presence in India’s neighbourhood (via ports and
Encirclement            infrastructure), aligning with the String of Pearls strategy and eroding India’s regional influence.
Environmental &         Projects in ecologically fragile regions (like Gilgit-Baltistan) often bypass environmental
Local Risks             assessments, threatening biodiversity, water security, and local livelihoods.
Undermining             BRI sidelines regional groupings like SAARC, BIMSTEC, and ASEAN by promoting bilateralism
Regional Forums         over multilateralism, weakening regional cooperation and India’s leadership role.

4.3. String of Pearls and Strategic Encirclement of India
                                                    String of Pearls
y The String of Pearls is a term that describes China’s strategic efforts to establish a network of military and commercial
  facilities across the Indian Ocean Region (IOR) and beyond.
y This includes ports, logistics bases, and infrastructure projects in countries such as Pakistan (Gwadar), Sri Lanka
  (Hambantota), Myanmar (Kyaukpyu), the Maldives, Bangladesh (Chittagong), and Djibouti (military base).

                                                    Impact on India
Strategic                    China is building ports around India, creating a "maritime ring" that can limit India’s influence
Encirclement                 and strategic space.
Security Threat              Increases risk of espionage and surveillance, as seen when Chinese submarines docked in Sri
                             Lanka in 2014.
Energy Route                 China's position at strategic chokepoints like Gwadar and the Strait of Malacca threatens
Vulnerability                India’s oil and trade flow routes.
Influence on                 China’s large investments and loans lead to debt-trap diplomacy, reducing India’s regional
Neighbours                   diplomatic clout.
                             Example: Sri Lanka leasing Hambantota to China.
Reduced Regional             China’s fast investments in India’s neighbourhood weaken India’s leadership in South Asia,
Leadership                   especially among smaller IOR nations.

                                             Countermeasures By India
Necklace of Diamonds         Building strategic ties and port access in friendly countries: Chabahar (Iran), Duqm (Oman),
Strategy                     Sittwe (Myanmar), Assumption (Seychelles).
SAGAR Vision                 India’s doctrine of Security and Growth for All in the Region promotes inclusive and
                             cooperative maritime security.
Indo-Pacific Pivot           Project Mausam & Sagarmala - Reconnecting ancient maritime trade routes and modernising
                             domestic port connectivity.
Maritime Diplomacy           Naval exercises and outreach such as Malabar (with the USA, Japan, and Australia),
                             MILAN, and training missions in Mauritius, the Maldives, and Seychelles.


Regional Cooperation         Staying active in QUAD, Indian Ocean Rim Association (IORA), BIMSTEC, and Indo-
Platforms                    Pacific Oceans Initiative (IPOI) to build alliances and resilience.
Maritime Information         IFC-IOR (Information Fusion Centre): Located in Gurugram, it shares real-time data with 25+
                             partner nations.


4.4. India-China Competition in Indo-Pacific and South China Sea
                    India–China Competition in the Indo-Pacific and South China Sea
y The Indo-Pacific region, particularly the South China Sea (SCS), has become a critical arena for strategic competition
  between India and China.
y While China seeks regional dominance through territorial claims, naval expansion, and the Belt and Road Initiative
  (BRI), India counters with its vision of a free, open, inclusive, and rules-based Indo-Pacific, guided by UNCLOS and
  multilateral partnerships.
y This competition reflects a deeper geopolitical contest over influence, trade routes, and maritime norms.

                           Significance of South China Sea & Indo-Pacific for India
Trade                  The SCS is India's "Maritime Life-Line." Over 55% of India's trade passes through these waters.
Security               Any disruption directly impacts the "Make in India" supply chain.
Energy                 The SCS holds estimated reserves of approximately 11 billion barrels of oil and 190 trillion cubic
Security               feet of natural gas. Access to these blocks is vital for India's energy diversification.


 Freedom of             Chinese militarisation and unilateral claims threaten FON in international waters. India upholds
 Navigation (FON)       UNCLOS principles and a rules-based maritime order.
 Geostrategic           Utilizing the necklace of diamonds and QUAD to prevent the SCS from becoming a Chinese lake,
 Balancing              ensuring a multipolar Indo-Pacific.

                     India’s Concerns Related to China’s Expansion in South China Sea
 UNCLOS & Legal         China’s rejection of the 2016 Permanent Court of Arbitration(PCA) ruling and the Ten-Dash Line
 Erosion                undermines the "Global Commons," threatening India's legal rights to high-seas passage
 Militarization         Increased naval deployments, surveillance infrastructure, and missiles threaten regional balance
 Risk                   and provoke arms buildup in Indo-Pacific countries.
 Cyber/Information      China's digital expansion under the Digital Silk Road and its control over undersea cables could
 Threats                expose India to espionage and cyber risks.
 ASEAN Pressure         China’s bilateral coercion of smaller ASEAN nations prevents a unified regional response, weakening
                        India’s Act East anchor.

4.5 China’s Mega Dam on the Tsangpo River: India’s Concern
China is constructing the world’s largest 60,000 MW hydropower project on the River Yarlung Tsangpo (River Brahmaputra)
at the Great Bend in Medog County, near Arunachal Pradesh. The project exploits the river’s steep descent to achieve carbon
neutrality, raising major strategic and ecological concerns for downstream India.

                                                     Concerns for India
 Strategic                China, as the upper riparian State, can manipulate water flow during geopolitical tensions, using
 Vulnerability            water as a strategic weapon.
 Livelihood               Chinese diversion or damming may severely impact irrigation, livelihoods, and drinking water in
 Impact                   Northeast India.
 Ecological Risk          Disruption of silt flow, potential biodiversity loss, and threats of flash floods due to construction in
                          fragile Himalayan terrain.
 Seismic Risk             The dam site is in a high-seismic zone; an earthquake or glacial lake outburst (the 2004 Parechu
                          Lake flood) could trigger catastrophic downstream flooding.
 Flood Management         The absence of real-time flood data from China limits India’s disaster preparedness, especially in
 Gaps                     Assam and Arunachal Pradesh during the monsoons.

                                            India’s Response to the Dispute
 Diplomatic               India has raised strong objections to unilateral dam construction by China during border talks
 Protests                 and through diplomatic channels.
 Hydropower               India is expediting hydropower and storage projects (Dibang, Upper Siang projects in Arunachal
 Development              Pradesh) to assert riparian rights.
 International            India highlights the issue in regional forums like BIMSTEC and bilateral cooperation with Nepal,
 Forums                   Bhutan, and Bangladesh for basin stability.
 Research & Satellite     Increased emphasis on hydrological mapping, satellite surveillance of Chinese dams, and climate-
 Monitoring               resilient planning in Northeast India.

                         Status of Coordination Mechanism on Transboundary Rivers
 Expert-Level             Set up for flood season data sharing on the Brahmaputra and Sutlej, but cooperation weakened
 Mechanism (2006)         after the Doklam and Galwan tensions.
 Brahmaputra &            China shares limited seasonal data (June–Oct); both MoUs have lapsed and exclude year-round
 Sutlej MoUs              or dam-specific information.
 2013 Umbrella MoU        A broad agreement on water cooperation has remained inactive, with no concrete technical
                          outcomes.

                                   Challenges for India in Resolving the Dispute
 Absence of a             Unlike the Indus Water Treaty, there is no legal framework with China; cooperation depends
 Binding Treaty           solely on diplomatic engagement and mutual goodwill.


No Legal Backing          Neither country is a signatory to the 1997 UN Watercourses Convention, limiting enforceable
                          international mechanisms for equitable water sharing.
Hydrological Data         China shares only partial and seasonal hydrological data; no obligation for real-time or dam-
Asymmetry                 construction updates.
China’s Unilateral        China’s record of unilateral dam-building on rivers like the Mekong heightens India’s fear of
Actions                   similar behaviour on the Brahmaputra.
Ecological and            Building mega dams in the seismically active and ecologically fragile Himalayas adds risks that
Seismic Complexity        require joint risk assessments, which are currently absent.

5. India–West Engagement to Reduce Dependence on China
     Dimension                              Main Strategy                                        Examples

                                           Economic Alternative to China
Promoting India as a         Western nations are encouraging production          Apple is assembling iPhones; Samsung is
Global Manufacturing         relocation from China to India to build resilient   shifting display manufacturing.
Hub                          supply chains, reducing overdependence.
Strengthening Trade          India is part of new-age tech-trade frameworks      US–India iCET ; India–EU TTC; role in
and Technology               to become a key link in global value chains.        Indo-Pacific Economic Framework.
Partnerships
Boosting Infrastructure      The West is funding India’s digital, energy, and    Build Back Better World (B3W); support for
& Investment                 transport infrastructure to counter China’s         solar parks, highways, and energy grids.
                             Belt and Road Initiative (BRI).
Green & Emerging             India is seen as a hub for clean energy, EVs,       Solar tech collaborations, a green hydrogen
Technologies                 and semiconductors to challenge China’s             push, and partnerships for chip
                             monopoly.                                           manufacturing with US firms.

                                         Political Dominance Against China
Strengthening Indo-          India is central to the West's Indo-Pacific         France officially co-leads the "Maritime
Pacific Security Role        strategy to ensure maritime stability and           Security" pillar of India’s Indo-Pacific
                             counter China.                                      Oceans Initiative (IPOI)
Expanding Defence &          Enhancing India’s military and space                GE jet engine deal, NISAR satellite (NASA–
Space Cooperation            capabilities to balance China’s growing             ISRO), and increased joint military drills.
                             assertiveness.
Supporting Global            Projecting India as a reliable democratic           The West strongly supports India's leadership
Democratic Leadership        counterweight to China in global governance.        in initiatives like the International Solar
                                                                                 Alliance and the Coalition for Disaster
                                                                                 Resilient Infrastructure (CDRI)
Strategic Trade &            Stronger bilateral and multilateral ties to build   Rising trade with the US, the India–EFTA
Diplomatic Alignment         India as a Western-aligned economic partner.        deal, and deeper ties with the G7.

6. Way Forward
        Way Forward                                                          Explanation
Institutionalised Dialogue &          Revive high-level talks like the Working Mechanism for Consultation and Coordination
Border Management                     on India-China Border Affairs (WMCC) and Special Representatives dialogue and
                                      enforce buffer zones and LAC protocols to prevent escalation and miscalculation.
Economic Rebalancing &                Reduce import dependence on China via Make in India, PLI schemes, and supply
Strategic Trade Diversification       chain diversification; address the trade deficit.
Military Modernisation & Border       Enhance deterrence through Rafale jets, ITBP deployment, Mountain Corps, and
Infrastructure Development            fast-track the Vibrant Villages Programme and BRO projects.
Crisis Management &                   Establish hotlines, develop grey-zone deterrents, boost cybersecurity, and pursue
Technological Sovereignty             technological sovereignty in critical areas like 5G and semiconductors.
Cultural Diplomacy & People-          Promote Track II/III diplomacy, student exchange, and cultural initiatives
to-People Confidence Building         (Yoga, Buddhism) to build long-term public goodwill and strategic trust.


 Geostrategic                          India should expedite the use of platforms like QUAD, I2U2, and Indo-Pacific Oceans
 Balancing                             Initiative (IPOI) to counter China's regional assertiveness and maintain constructive
                                       engagement in the SCO, BRICS, and RIC frameworks.
 Deepening strategic ties              Deepening strategic ties with Vietnam, the Philippines, and Indonesia to expand
                                       military logistics, port access, and naval interoperability.
 Naval Footprint & Power               Regular bilateral/multilateral naval drills (Malabar, SIMBEX) showcase India’s
 Projection                            ability to ensure peace, deterrence, and stability in the region.
 Climate Change &                      Despite strategic competition, India and China should cooperate on global climate
 Environmental Cooperation             governance, renewable energy transition, disaster-risk reduction, glacier monitoring, and
                                       sustainable development.
India–China relations require strategic competition with calibrated cooperation, guided by mutual respect, mutual sensitivity,
and mutual interests. Strengthening border stability, confidence-building measures (CBMs), economic resilience, and multilateral
engagement is essential for long-term peace. A balanced approach based on strategic autonomy, deterrence, dialogue, and rules-
based order will shape a stable Asian century

chapters.text:
8              India - Bhutan Relations

India–Bhutan relations represent a model of friendship rooted in trust, mutual respect, and strategic cooperation. Anchored
by the 1949 Treaty of Friendship, revised in 2007, the partnership spans diverse areas like hydropower, connectivity, security,
culture, and people-to-people ties, reflecting India's Neighbourhood First policy and Bhutan’s emphasis on sovereign yet
close ties with India.


1. Area of Cooperation
        Area                             Description                                            Examples
 Strategic &            Joint management of the Siliguri Corridor           Joint action against ULFA (United Liberation Front
 Security               (Chicken's Neck) and coordinated border             of Asom) & NDFB (National Democratic Front of
 Partnership            security.                                           Boroland).
 Sovereignty &          Adherence to the 2007 Revised Treaty of             Affirmation of Bhutan's independent foreign policy
 Political Linkages     Friendship, emphasizing non-interference and        while coordinating on regional security threats (e.g.
                        strategic consultation.                             Doklam/Border talks)
 Hydropower &           Transition from Buyer-Seller to a Strategic         Signing of Agreement on the Punatsangchhu-II
 Energy                 Energy Partnership, aiming for 10,000 MW            Hydroelectric Project (PHEP-II) .
 Sovereignty            capacity.
 Economic & Trade       Full Free Trade Regime (2016 Agreement)             Since 2014, India’s trade with Bhutan has
 Integration            allowing duty-free transit for Bhutanese goods      more than tripled, accounting for over 80%
                        to third countries via Indian ports.                of Bhutan’s overall trade.
 Development &          Integration of Bhutan into the Indian Multi-        Project DANTAK roads; Kokrajhar–Gelephu rail
 Connectivity           Modal Network (Rail, Road, and Waterways).          link.
 Support
 Environmental          Joint management of contiguous ecological           Transboundary Manas Conservation Area
 Cooperation            zones to secure migratory corridors for             (TraMCA) for wildlife conservation
                        "umbrella species" (tigers/elephants).
 Technological          Bhutan uses indigenous satellite data for forest    Utilising the India-Bhutan SAT (2022) ground
 Cooperation            fire alerts and agricultural yield estimation via   station in Thimphu for governance and resource
                        the ISRO-developed ground station.                  mapping.
 Cultural &             Shared Buddhist heritage and scholarship            Nehru-Wangchuck Scholarship.
 Educational            programmes reinforce soft power and
 Engagement             people-to-people ties.


2. Significance of India-Bhutan Bilateral Relations
         Area                                                             Significance
 Strategic Buffer         y The 2017 Doklam standoff highlighted Bhutan’s role in helping India counter Chinese
 Against China              expansion near the Chumbi Valley tri-junction, protecting the Siliguri Corridor.
 Development &            y India supports Bhutan’s 13th Five-Year Plan (2024–29) and Economic Stimulus Programme
 Financial Assistance       through budgetary aid, technical support, and project funding in key sectors.
 Energy Partnership       y Bhutan has a massive, largely untapped hydropower potential of approximately 30,000 to 40,000
                            MW, with roughly 2,500 to 3000 MW currently operational, mostly developed with Indian
                            support.
                          y The electricity trade volume reached approximately 5,600 GWh (5.6 billion units) in 2021.
                          y On 11 November 2025, India and Bhutan jointly inaugurated the 1020 MW Punatsangchhu-II
                            power plant.
 Trade significance       y India accounts for over 80% of Bhutan's total trade, providing a stable market for exports and
                            essential imports
 Connectivity &           y India’s BRO has developed key infrastructure like Paro Airport under Project DANTAK and
 Digital Cooperation        backs Bhutan’s Digital Drukyul for e-governance.
 Defence & Regional       y The Indian Military Training Team (IMTRAT) provides military training to Bhutanese forces
 Cooperation                and ensures border security coordination.
                          y Both countries cooperate in regional forums like BBIN and BIMSTEC.

3. Growing Concerns in India-Bhutan Relations
    Challenge                       Description                                                 Example
 China’s Strategic      Chinese activities near Bhutan’s border         China and Bhutan discussed "boundary alignment in
 Presence               undermine India’s strategic depth and           undisputed areas" in Beijing, raising Indian concerns over a
                        threaten the Siliguri Corridor.                 potential land swap involving the Doklam tri-junction.
 Hydropower             Delays and pricing disputes in India-           Despite the dam restart, the 7-year suspension of
 Infrastructure         backed hydropower projects have                 Punatsangchhu-I has caused significant debt-servicing
 Issues                 caused resentment in Bhutan.                    anxiety in Thimphu.
 Trade                  India's concern that Bhutan's efforts to        Bhutan's negotiations with China on the Jakarlung–
 Diversification        diversify trade and reduce dependence           Pasamlung border settlement package and its push to
 Pressure               on India could increase Chinese                 attract non-Indian investment through the Gelephu
                        economic influence.                             Mindfulness City project.
 Labour                 Bhutan imposed tighter rules on                 Post-2020: Reduced entry permits and stricter regulations.
 Restrictions           Indian workers and traders, citing
                        local strain.

3.1. India–Bhutan–China Triangle
The India–Bhutan–China Triangle encapsulates the evolving geopolitical dynamics of the eastern Himalayas. While India shares
deep strategic and historical ties with Bhutan, China’s increasing economic and territorial influence is reshaping Bhutan's
foreign engagements.


                                Strategic Developments in China–Bhutan Relations
 Chinese                 Chinese State-Owned Enterprises (SOEs) are involved in strategic sectors, including hydropower
 Footprint               (Chukha, Punatsangchhu).
 Digital                 Chinese firms are expanding telecom and internet networks, raising concerns over cybersecurity,
 Infrastructure          data sovereignty, and Bhutan’s digital dependence.
 Influence
 Territorial             China claims territories in Bhutan’s North (Jakarlung, Pasamlung) and West (Doklam). In 2020, it
 Assertion               extended claims to Sakteng Wildlife Sanctuary (East) and constructed Pangda village.
 Diplomatic              Bhutan’s first foreign ministerial visit to China (2023) resulted in a 3-step roadmap for boundary
 Outreach                talks, a sign of growing formal engagement.

                                                   Implications for India
 Strategic               Chinese presence near Doklam Plateau threatens the Siliguri Corridor (India’s lifeline to the
 Vulnerability           Northeast), risking a strategic choke point.
 Security Concerns       PLA buildup near Bhutan raises alarms for India’s eastern defence, especially near Arunachal
 in Northeast            Pradesh, which China claims as "South Tibet".
 India–China             Bhutan’s tilt towards balancing China and India may shrink India’s diplomatic space and deepen
 Rivalry Escalation      India–China contestation in regional forums.

4. Way Forward
 Strategic Autonomy         Adopting a non-intrusive engagement model and deepening inter-parliamentary cooperation
 & Diplomacy                and supporting its role in Neighbourhood First & Act East policies → India's backing of Gelephu
                            Mindfulness City (GMC) is a strategic move to ensure Bhutan’s diversification remains anchored
                            to Indian investments
 Diversified Economic       Promote trade in fintech, space tech, tourism, and the services sector in line with Bhutan’s Gross
 Partnership                National Happiness (GNH) model.
 Treaty Revision &          Periodically reviewing the 2007 Treaty of Friendship to ensure it reflects Bhutan’s status as an
 Sovereignty                equal, sovereign partner while maintaining the indestructible security bond.
 Hydropower &               Fast-track pending hydro projects to meet the 10,000 MW goal; ensure timely execution, fair
 Energy Cooperation         tariffs, and explore clean energy partnerships.
 Countering China’s         Assist Bhutan in managing border talks with China (3-step roadmap); consider an India–
 Influence                  Bhutan–China trilogue to reduce strategic ambiguity.
 Defence & Border           Enhance joint military exercises and real-time information sharing on insurgent threats, and
 Security                   strengthen air defence and border surveillance.
India–Bhutan relations represent a unique example of neighbourhood diplomacy built on trust, mutual respect, and shared
developmental goals. Anchored in enduring friendship and guided by sensitivity to each other’s strategic interests, the partnership
between India and Bhutan continues to evolve as a model of cooperative regionalism in South Asia.

9              India - Sri Lanka Relations

India and Sri Lanka share a civilisational bond spanning over 2,500 years, rooted in a common Buddhist heritage, linguistic
ties, and cultural affinity. Their contemporary partnership spans strategic, economic, defence and people-to-people cooperation.
India’s timely support during Sri Lanka’s 2022 economic crisis reaffirmed its commitment to the Neighbourhood First and
MAHASAGAR visions, especially amid China’s growing influence in the region.


                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    India is an age-old friend of Sri Lanka. Discuss India’s role in the recent crisis in Sri
            Lanka in light of the preceding statement. [2022]
      2.    In respect of India —Sri Lanka relations, discuss how domestic factors influence foreign
            policy. [2013]


Evolution of India-Sri Lanka Relations


1. Area of Cooperation Between India and Sri Lanka (SL)
        Area                     Key Aspects                                            Examples
Economic           India’s cooperation spans from economic         In 2025, India and Sri Lanka restructured nearly
Cooperation        bailouts to supporting housing, education,      US$931 million in Indian Lines of Credit and Buye rs'
                   healthcare, transport, and infrastructure       Credit facilities (Indian Housing Project, Suwa Seriya
                                                                   Ambulance Service).

                   training, underpinned by India's                y The Sri Lankan Maritime Rescue Coordination
                   "Neighbourhood First" policy and shared           Centre (MRCC) was established through India’s
                   security interests. Also, bilateral exercises     funding.
                   are held annually.

and Digital        digital connectivity with Sri Lanka, linking    resumed between Nagapattinam (India) and
Cooperation        it with India’s Digital Public Infrastructure   Kankesanthurai (Sri Lanka).
                   (DPI).
Religious &        Bilateral cooperation is rooted in              Display of Buddha relics on Vesak Day (2025), launch
Cultural           Buddhism, Hinduism, and Tamil culture.          of the Ramayana Trail, and presence of the Indian
                                                                   Cultural Centre in Colombo.

2. Significance of India-Sri Lanka Relations
   Aspect                                                  Key Significance
Strategic      y The island’s position near vital Sea Lanes of Communication (SLOCs), through which almost two-thirds
                 of global oil shipments and nearly half of international container traffic pass, gives it unparalleled
                 strategic value.
Trade          y India is Sri Lanka’s largest trading partner and a top source of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) →
                 Operates under the India–Sri Lanka Free Trade Agreement (ISFTA).
               y Example: The merchandise trade between India and Sri Lanka amounted to USD 5.54 billion in FY
                 2023-24, with India’s exports at USD 4.11 billion and Sri Lanka’s exports at USD 1.42 billion.


Maritime            y Positioned only ~30 km from India's coast, Sri Lanka is vital for controlling Indian Ocean traffic → It
Security              will ensure regional surveillance, preventing hostile power intrusion, and curbing non-traditional threats
                      like drug trafficking.
                    y Example: SLINEX (Sri Lanka–India Naval Exercise), Mitra Shakti (Army exercise).
Chinese             y The rise of China’s influence in Sri Lanka has significantly heightened the island’s geo-strategic
Threat                importance for India.
                    y Example: The 99-year lease of the Hambantota Port to a Chinese company symbolised the possibility of
                      China gaining a long-term strategic foothold in India’s maritime neighbourhood.
Energy &            y Strengthens integration via the Trincomalee energy hub, proposed undersea power grid,
Connectivity          Nagapattinam-Kankesanthurai ferry, and port infrastructure upgrades.
Indian              y India supports political inclusion and power devolution for ~1.6 million Indian-origin Tamils through
Diaspora              advocating the 13th Amendment, which is significant for soft diplomacy and people-to-people ties.

2.1. India’s role in the recent crisis in Sri Lanka

       Area                                                           Description
Financial &               India provided ~USD 4 billion (credit lines, currency swaps, loan deferments) to avert humanitarian
Economic Aid              collapse → key to stabilising Sri Lanka’s economy.
IMF Bailout               India gave early financial assurances to the IMF, enabling the USD 2.9 billion bailout in 2023 →
Facilitation              demonstrating India’s diplomatic agility.
Humanitarian              y During the COVID-19 crisis, India sent vaccines, food, and medical aid.
Assistance                y In late 2025 and early 2026, India launched Operation Sagar Bandhu, providing over 1,000
                            tonnes of emergency humanitarian aid and disaster relief (HADR) to Sri Lanka following the
                            devastation caused by Cyclone Ditwah.
                          y It reaffirms India’s role as a ‘first responder’ in the region.
Infrastructure &          India supported inclusive recovery via housing projects for the Indian-origin Tamils,
Development               Kankesanthurai Port and emergency health services, thus advancing post-conflict development.
Projects
Political &               India has advocated Tamil political rights through the 13th Amendment and sustained dialogue via
Diplomatic                the India–Sri Lanka Joint Commission.
Engagement
Maritime &                India has trained Sri Lankan forces, provided a Floating Dock Facility, supported the Maritime
Security                  Rescue Coordination Centre (MRCC), and enhanced regional security through the Colombo
Cooperation               Security Conclave.

3. Challenges in India-Sri Lanka Relations
    Challenge                                                         Description
Tamil Issue & 13th         Sri Lanka has not fully implemented the 13th Amendment promised under the 1987 Indo-Lanka
Amendment                  Accord, delaying political decentralization for Tamil-majority areas.
China’s Strategic          China’s growing role through projects like Hambantota Port and the docking of PLA surveillance
Presence                   ships poses a direct security challenge to India in the Indian Ocean Region (IOR).
Fishermen                  Indian fishermen crossing into Sri Lankan waters often face arrests and boat seizures. Use of
Dispute                    bottom trawling by Indian boats is an environmental and livelihood threat to Sri Lankan coastal
                           communities.
Katchatheevu               Though ceded to Sri Lanka in 1974, Katchatheevu remains a politically sensitive issue in Tamil
Island Issue               Nadu, often linked with the fishermen dispute and raised in domestic political debates.
Trade Imbalance            India holds a significant trade surplus over Sri Lanka. Sri Lanka demands greater market access
                           and a more balanced trade relationship.


4. Way Forward
       Focus Area                                                        Explanation
 Institutional Dialogue        Set up a permanent bilateral framework (Joint Fisheries Commission) to address fishermen's
 on Fisheries                  disputes, promote sustainable practices, and prevent repeated arrests.
 Strategic Energy and          Expedite key projects like the Trincomalee energy hub, India–Sri Lanka undersea power
 Connectivity Projects         grid, and port infrastructure development to enhance mutual energy security.
 Reconciliation through        Use developmental aid, skills programmes, and cultural linkages to indirectly support the
 Targeted Support              implementation of the 13th Amendment, ensuring progress on Tamil reconciliation.
 Multilateral                  Strengthen cooperation through BIMSTEC, Colombo Security Conclave, and IORA on issues
 Cooperation                   like maritime security, economic recovery, and climate resilience.
 Comprehensive Security        MAHASAGAR Initiative: Implementing real-time intelligence sharing on Dual-Use vessel
 Framework                     movements and operationalising the India-funded Maritime Rescue Coordination Centre
                               (MRCC)
 Spiritual & Cultural          Leveraging shared Buddhist/Hindu heritage via the Buddhist Circuit and Ramayana Trail to
 Diplomacy                     reinforce civilizational ties and counter external soft-power narratives.
 Countering the China          Offering high-quality, low-debt infrastructure alternatives and institutionalising security
 Factor                        protocols to monitor dual-use Chinese assets.
 Sports Diplomacy &            Leveraging high-profile events like the 2026 ICC Men’s T20 World Cup (co-hosted by India &
 Youth                         SL) to build joint tourism circuits and strengthen regional identity through sports.
 Climate Change                Enhance cooperation on coastal resilience, marine ecosystem conservation, sustainable
 Partnership                   fisheries, renewable energy, and climate adaptation to address shared vulnerabilities arising
                               from sea-level rise and extreme weather events
India-Sri Lanka relations are anchored by deep-rooted civilizational ties, strategic geographic proximity, and India's proactive
"Neighbourhood First" policy. Despite occasional friction over fishing rights and geopolitical balancing, recent economic support
during crises has reinforced India as a critical, stable partner. Moving forward, the relationship relies on deepening economic
integration, maritime security cooperation, and balancing mutual regional security interests.


  Bilateral Groupings

 Agreements Involving
India and / or Affecting
   India’s Interests.

chapters.text:
1              India - USA Relations

India–USA relations have transformed from estranged democracies to a Comprehensive Global Strategic Partnership,
driven by shared democratic values, strategic convergence, and technological collaboration. Today, the partnership is a
key pillar of the Indo-Pacific architecture, encompassing defence, trade, innovation, and global governance, while navigating
emerging policy divergences.

                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    “The USA is facing an existential threat in the form of China that is much more
            challenging than the erstwhile Soviet Union.” Explain. (2021)
      2.    What is the significance of Indo-US defence deals over Indo-Russian defence deals?
            Discuss with reference to stability in the Indo-Pacific region. (2020)
      3.    “What introduces friction into the ties between India and the United States is that
            Washington is still unable to find for India a position in its global strategy, which
            would satisfy India’s National self- esteem and ambitions” Explain with suitable
            examples. (2019)

      Aspect                                                            Description

India and the United States, while pursuing independent strategic interests, continue to deepen cooperation based on mutual
trust and shared values. Their partnership will remain central to promoting global stability, technological innovation, and a
free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
2             India-Russia Relations

India–Russia relations, rooted in deep historical trust and strategic convergence, have evolved into a Special and Privileged Strategic
Partnership encompassing defence, energy, trade, and technology cooperation. Despite shifting global dynamics, the partnership
remains resilient, driven by mutual interests in strategic autonomy, multipolarity, and regional stability, with Russia continuing as a
key defence partner and energy supplier for India.
                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Russia and Ukraine war had been going on for the last seven months. What ethical
            issues are crucial to consider while launching the war and its continuation so far?
            Illustrate with justification the ethical issues involved in the given state of affairs. (2022)
      2.    How is the S-400 air defence system technically superior to any other system presently
            available in the world? (2021)
      3.    What is the significance of Indo-US deals over Indo-Russian defence deals? Discuss with
            reference to stability in the Indo-Pacific region. (2020).


Areas of Cooperation
     Area                             Description                                                 Example
 Trade             Expanding beyond oil to fertilisers and minerals,       Bilateral trade hit a record high with Russia increasing
                   working toward the $100 billion bilateral trade         fertiliser exports to India by 40%.
                   target by 2030.
 Energy            Cooperation spans hydrocarbons and civilian             Atommash of Russia had sent the reactor pressure
                   nuclear projects to strengthen India’s long-term        vessel for Kudankulam Nuclear Plant Unit 6.
                   energy security.
 Cultural          People-to-people ties are enhanced through              "Namaste Russia" is a major, recurring festival of
                   cultural festivals, exchange programmes, and            Indian culture in Russia designed to strengthen cultural
                   language promotion initiatives.                         ties through dance, music, cinema, and yoga.
 Space             Russia supports India in its space missions through     Training of Gaganyaan astronauts at the Yuri
                   astronaut training and technology transfer for          Gagarin Cosmonaut Centre.
                   space navigation systems.

Significance of India-Russia Relations
The India-Russia Special and Privileged Strategic Partnership has remained stable and resilient despite global geopolitical challenges.

                      energy, and geopolitical influence.                           framework to create multipolar structures.

Current Status of India-Russia Relations in a Changing Global Order
India–Russia relations remain robust, anchored in defence, energy, and strategic cooperation, even as both adapt to shifting global
power equations. Amid sanctions and realignments, the partnership emphasises multipolarity, connectivity, and economic diversification
to sustain relevance in a competitive world order.

       Aspect                                 Description                                             Example

China–Pakistan–Russia Alignment
The emerging China–Pakistan–Russia alignment reflects a strategic convergence aimed at reducing Western influence and reshaping
regional security architecture. This trilateral synergy constrains India’s strategic space by limiting its role in Afghan peace processes,
regional connectivity, and Eurasian geopolitics.

The China–Pakistan–Russia (CPR) Alignment
      Aspect                                                             Description

 Mediation                to the Western-led initiatives.

 Pressure                 monopoly that challenges India's INSTC.

                       fighter jets.

 Erosion                  counter-terrorism narratives.

                                                                                  enhancing interoperability.

 Mechanism                                                                        reducing costs.

Russia-Ukraine War
The Russia–Ukraine conflict, which began in February 2022, has reshaped global geopolitics, trade, and security dynamics. For India,
it poses both challenges and opportunities, testing its principle of strategic autonomy.


India’s Stance
       Aspect                               Description                                              Example

 Diplomacy                of hostilities, dialogue & respect for                 condemning Russia.

                          (RELOS) with Russia to secure Arctic interests         Polar-vessel project with Russia's Rosatom.

Role of the Act Far East Policy in India-Russia Relations
India’s Act Far East Policy is a strategic initiative to deepen trade, investment, connectivity, and economic cooperation with Russia’s
Far East. It focuses on developing new trade corridors, attracting investments, and forging partnerships for mutual growth and
resource-driven collaboration.


        Aspect                                  Description                                               Example
 Strategic Pivot            India's Act Far East policy aligns with Russia’s            India’s PM visited Vladivostok for the first

                            enhancing strategic depth beyond traditional                strategic focus.
                            Moscow-centric ties.

                            on West Asia.                                               East.

                                                                                        carrying oil, food and machines.

                            trade diversification.

                            and strengthening multipolarity.

Way Forward
           Aspect                                                             Description

                                  mutual benefits and reduce the trade imbalance.

                                  strengthen India's long-term energy security..

                                  BrahMos missiles, and advanced military platforms under the Make in India framework.

 Cooperation                      engagement with Russia and China to balance regional dynamics.

 Innovation                       oriented cooperation.

India-Russia relations remain a cornerstone of strategic stability, marked by enduring cooperation in defence, energy, and technology,
fostering a multipolar world order. As a testament to this timeless bond, it is often said, "One old friend is better than two new friends,"
reflecting the depth of trust and mutual understanding between the two nations.

How is the S-400 air defence system technically superior to any other system presently available in the world? (2021)
What is the significance of Indo-US deals over Indo-Russian defence deals? Discuss with reference to stability in the Indo-Pacific region. (2020)

chapters.text:
3              India - EU Relations

India–EU relations have evolved into a multi-dimensional Strategic Partnership, anchored in shared democratic values, rules-
based multilateralism, and economic complementarities. Spanning trade, technology, climate action, connectivity, and Indo-
Pacific cooperation, the partnership has gained renewed momentum with the India–EU Free Trade Agreement (2026), strengthening
geo-economic integration and strategic convergence.


Areas of Cooperation between India and the EU
     Aspect                             Explanation                                            Example
 Security &          India and the EU collaborate on maritime security,     India and the EU conducted a joint exercise in the
 Defence             counterterrorism, and cyber resilience to ensure       Gulf of Guinea (2023) and took part in
                     stability in the Indo-Pacific.                         EUNAVFOR Atalanta. (2025)
 Climate &           Partnership focuses on renewable energy, green         The EU-India Clean Energy and Climate
 Energy              hydrogen, and climate action to meet sustainable       Partnership (CECP) (2016)
                     development goals.
 Connectivity        Enhancing transport and economic links through         EU is a core partner & a foundational signatory of
                     strategic corridors to boost trade and bypass          the India-Middle East - Europe Economic
                     chokepoints.                                           Corridor (IMEC)
 Technology &        Cooperation on AI, semiconductors, and cyber           Signed the Digital Sovereignty Pact under the
 Digital             norms through strategic technology councils to         recent FTA, ensuring that Indian source code
                     strengthen digital governance.                         remains protected during joint AI ventures with
                                                                            EU firms.
 Science &           Joint projects in research and satellite missions to   ISRO & ESA hosted a Joint Heliophysics
 Space               advance scientific innovation.                         Workshop to integrate real-time solar wind data
                                                                            from Aditya-L1, Solar Orbiter & Proba-3 for
                                                                            improved space-weather forecasting.

                     advanced reactor development to ensure energy          Community and India on R&D cooperation for the
                     security.                                              peaceful use of nuclear energy. (2020)

                   workforce benefits.                                       implemented to enhance structured migration.

Agriculture        standards, and sustainable farming practices for          in Hyderabad to secure API (active
                   global health security.                                   pharmaceutical ingredient) supply chains. (2026)

Significance of India-EU Relations
    Aspect                           Explanation                                               Example

                                                          For India

Innovation           blocs for high-end tech.                             was launched to deepen technological cooperation.

cooperation          their positions across multilateral platforms,       drive WTO reforms, especially restoring the dispute
                     driven by shared values of democracy and a           settlement system.
                     rules-based international order.

Sustainability       through funding green projects in various            package from the EU over two years to assist India in

& Stability          Ocean, protecting critical European trade routes.    Ocean.

                trade measures and tariffs                           Agreement.

India-EU FTA (2026)


Features of FTA


    Feature                                                          Details
Economic Scale    y Covers 2 billion people, 25% of global GDP, and 1/3rd of global trade.
Tariff            y India has gained preferential access to the European markets across 97% of tariff lines, covering
Liberalisation      99.5% of trade value
                  y India offered 92.1% of its tariff lines, which covers 97.5% of the EU exports.
Key Indian        y Textiles, leather, footwear, gems & jewellery, marine products, and tea/coffee.
Gainers
Automobile        y Calibrated Liberalisation: India cuts car duties from 110% to 10% over 5 years, but only for a
Quota               limited quota of luxury EVs/cars.
Services          y Access to 144 EU subsectors. Includes fast-track visas for IT professionals and recognition of
(Mode 4)            AYUSH practitioners.
Agriculture       y Sensitives Excluded: Dairy, Wheat, Rice, Poultry, and Sugar are kept out of the deal to protect Indian
                    farmers.
Pharma / IPR      y FTA reinforces TRIPS-based IPRs (copyrights, trademarks, designs, trade secrets, plant varieties),
                    affirms the Doha Declaration, and recognises India's Traditional Knowledge Digital Library
                    (TKDL).
Sustainability    y Binding Trade & Sustainable Development (TSD) chapter. Includes labour rights (ILO), gender
                    equality, and environmental protection.
Carbon            y India secured MFN (Most-Favoured-Nation) assurance; any flexibility the EU gives to others on
(CBAM)              the "Carbon Tax" must be given to India.

Challenges in the India–EU FTA (2026)
   Challenge                            Description                                            Example
CBAM                The EU’s Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism                Indian steel which typically carries 2.3-2.8
(Carbon Tax)        entered its financial phase on Jan 1, 2026. It acts as a   tonnes of CO₂ for every tonne produced, will
Shock               green tariff that erodes the FTA's duty-free benefits.     face a significant cost hit.
TSD Sovereignty     The Trade & Sustainable Development (TSD) allows           EU monitors flagged labour conditions in
Friction            the EU to monitor Indian labour and environmental          Bengaluru's tech sector, sparking a
                    laws, which India views as regulatory imperialism.         cooperative dispute under the FTA.


 Non-Tariff              Complex EU quality standards often act as invisible        Indian coffee and wood exports are struggling
 Barriers (NTBs)         walls that block Indian agricultural and chemical          with the EU Deforestation Regulation
                         exports.                                                   (EUDR) geolocation requirements.
 Source Code & AI        The FTA prohibits India from demanding access to           FTA restricts access to EU AI source code,
 Limits                  software source code, potentially limiting India’s         limiting India’s ability to audit algorithms for
                         ability to audit high-risk AI algorithms.                  bias or safety before deployment.

Way Forward
            Aspect                                                          Description
 Operationalizing the FTA          Transitioning from legal text to a Single Window for businesses to utilise zero-duty benefits.
 Regulatory Convergence            Harmonising Indian standards with EU norms to bypass non-tariff barriers without
                                   sacrificing domestic policy space.
 Climate Synergy (Anti-            Leveraging EU green funding to decarbonise Indian heavy industry, turning the carbon tax
 CBAM)                             challenge into a green premium opportunity.
 Strategic Autonomy                Moving toward joint co-development of defense tech to reduce dependence on individual
                                   nations for critical components.
 Mobility & Talent Hub             Utilising the new talent passport to create a legal, circular migration corridor for India’s
                                   STEM workforce.
 Sovereign Non-Interference        Ensuring the Trade & Sustainable Development (TSD) chapter remains a consultative
                                   platform rather than a tool for sanctioning Indian policy.
 Climate Change                    Expand cooperation under the India-EU Trade and Technology Council and joint initiatives
                                   on green hydrogen, offshore wind energy, and sustainable infrastructure financing

The partnership between India and the European Union is steadily evolving into a convergence of democracy, development, and
strategic stability in a fragmented world order. India–EU relations are like two parallel rivers gradually bending toward the same
ocean of multipolarity, distinct in origin but increasingly unified in purpose and direction.

chapters.text:
4              India - UK Relations

India–UK relations have evolved into a Comprehensive Strategic Partnership focused on a free Indo-Pacific and industrial integration.
The Roadmap 2030 and the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA) of 2025 drive the partnership beyond its
historical ties. This future-ready bond is now centered on implementing CETA and advancing the Technology Security Initiative in
AI, semiconductors, and green energy.

Evolution of India-UK Relations


Areas of Cooperation in India-UK Relations
       Aspect                                Description                                             Example
 Economic & Trade        Stronger economic ties through the India-UK Joint        Current trade between both countries is worth
 Partnership             Economic and Trade Committee (JETCO); CETA is            £47.2 billion and the trade deal is forecast to
                         signed to enhance trade, investment, and supply          boost bilateral trade by £25.5 billion yearly.
                         chain resilience.
 Defence &               Transition from buyer-seller to co-development           Framework agreement to co-design and
 Security                under the Vision 2035 roadmap.                           co-produce electric propulsion systems to be
 Cooperation                                                                      used in futuristic warships.
 Civil Nuclear           Both nations cooperate in the peaceful use of nuclear    The 2020 Nuclear Collaboration Agreement
 Collaboration           energy                                                   supports peaceful energy cooperation and R&D.
 Education & Skill       The UK supports Indian students and institutional        The University of Southampton opens India’s
 Development             ties; both collaborate in research, mobility, and        first UK branch in Gurugram.
                         mutual recognition.
 Health & Pharma         Joint efforts in vaccines, digital health, Ayurveda,     Oxford - AstraZeneca partnered with the
 Cooperation             AMR, and health workforce exchanges post-                Serum Institute for global distribution of
                         COVID.                                                   Covishield.
 Climate & Green         Cooperation on renewables, EVs, and climate              Green Growth Equity Fund invests in India’s
 Growth                  finance through tech sharing and green investment        clean energy, waste, and EV sectors.
                         platforms.
 Cultural                Boosting cultural diplomacy via MoUs, literature,        The Nehru Centre in London actively
 Engagement              art, and institutional linkages like libraries and       promotes Indian culture through events and
                         cultural centres.                                        outreach.

      Aspect                              Description                                             Example

                        Agreement (CETA) aims to double bilateral trade        and Scotch whisky under CETA.

India–UK Vision 2035
India-UK Vision 2035 outlines a long-term strategic roadmap to elevate bilateral ties across defence, technology, trade, and
global governance. It reflects a shared commitment to a free, open, and rules-based international order amidst evolving
geopolitical challenges.


Features of India–UK Vision 2035


Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA)
India and the United Kingdom have signed the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA), a bilateral free trade
agreement marking a major milestone in their longstanding partnership.

Key Features of CETA
      Aspect                                                           Description
 Duty-Free Access        99% duty-free access to UK market for Indian goods, benefiting textiles, leather, EVs, and auto parts.

                         edible oils to protect rural livelihoods.
 IPR & Pharma            No data exclusivity; protects India’s generic pharmaceutical industry.

 Cooperation             extremism.

India–UK relations are steadily deepening as both countries find common ground on trade, security, and global challenges.
Strengthened by the vibrant “Living Bridge” of people-to-people ties, the partnership is transforming into a dynamic alliance
focused on shared growth and global collaboration.

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
5            India - Japan Relations

India–Japan relations constitute a Special Strategic and Global Partnership founded on shared democratic values, mutual
trust, and a common Indo-Pacific vision. Driven by high-quality infrastructure, economic security, technological
collaboration, supply-chain resilience, and maritime cooperation, the partnership is a key pillar of India’s Act East Policy
and Indo-Pacific strategy.
                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    The time has come for India and Japan to build a strong contemporary relationship, one
           involving a global and strategic partnership that will have a great significance for Asia
           and the world as a whole. Comment. (2019)
     2.    Economic ties between India and Japan, while growing in recent years, are still far below
           their potential. Elucidate the policy constraints which are inhibiting this growth. (2013)


Evolution of India-Japan Relations

Way Forward

In an era of geopolitical contestation, India and Japan are natural partners in advancing a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
As India's rise converges with Japan's evolving strategic role, their partnership is set to strengthen regional stability, prosperity, and
21st-century global cooperation.
pyq: The time has come for India and Japan to build a strong contemporary relationship, one involving a global and strategic partnership that will have a great significance for Asia and the world as a whole. Comment. (2019)
Economic ties between India and Japan, while growing in recent years, are still far below their potential. Elucidate the policy constraints which are inhibiting this growth. (2013)

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
6            India - Australia Relations

India–Australia relations have evolved into a Comprehensive Strategic Partnership (2020), rooted in early colonial trade links and
the establishment of India’s Trade Office in Sydney in 1941. Based on shared democratic values, Commonwealth traditions, and
Indo-Pacific convergence, the partnership now spans defence, trade, critical minerals, education, and strong people-to-people ties,
supported by frameworks like the Economic Cooperation and Trade Agreement, QUAD, and the Critical Minerals Investment
Partnership.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    The time has come for India and Japan to build a strong contemporary relationship, one
            involving a global and strategic partnership that will have a great significance for Asia
            and the world as a whole. Comment. (2019)
      2.    Economic ties between India and Japan, while growing in recent years, are still far below
            their potential. Elucidate the policy constraints which are inhibiting this growth. (2013)


Area of Cooperation
       Area                                  Key Points                                             Example
 Diplomatic            Relations are a Comprehensive Strategic Partnership         India–Australia cooperation through the
                       based on shared values, trust, and a free, open Indo-       Quad and the regular 2+2 Ministerial

                                                                                   strategic convergence in the Indo-Pacific

                       strengthen cultural and educational ties.                   student and professional mobility.

                                                                                      cobalt, and rare earths.

                            energy generation.                                     world's third-largest producer of uranium.

                            and economic engagement.                               population).

Challenges in India-Australia Relationships
   Challenge                                                            Key Points
Trade & Market          While the interim ECTA is successful, the full CECA remains blocked by sensitive sectors like dairy
Access                  and digital trade.

Extremism               The Khalistani movement in Australia is a source of worry for the Indian diaspora and also in bilateral

Gap                     about whether it’s profitable for India.

                        40% of Australian student visa applications from India are being rejected. (LEAMSS study)

Differences             more focused on maritime threats. Australia also works closely with the US under AUKUS.
Global Issue            In the Russia–Ukraine war, India stayed neutral while Australia supported the US and its allies,
Differences             causing some disagreements.


Critical Minerals Investment Partnership (CMIP)
CMIP was launched in March 2022 through an MoU between KABIL and CMO Australia and seeks to build secure and
resilient supply chains, cut dependence on China and meet clean energy and manufacturing needs.

 Security                   wind, and green hydrogen infrastructure,                National Hydrogen Mission projects.

                            minerals.                                               equipment production.

Way Forward
        Area                                                         Description
 Strengthen             Aim for $50 billion in bilateral trade by 2030 by diversifying into textiles, pharma, and premium
 Trade                  agri-products.
 Enhance                Expand cooperation in maritime domain awareness, cyber security, counter-terrorism, and defence

                        strengthen Indo-Pacific deterrence capabilities

 Collaboration          groups to promote regional stability, infrastructure development, and strategic balance in the
                        Indo-Pacific.
 Tackle                 Improve intelligence sharing, set up joint monitoring systems, and engage with diaspora
 Extremism              communities to counter extremism and hate crimes.
 Renewable Energy       Scale cooperation in green hydrogen, solar, batteries, and critical minerals; joint investments will
 Partnership            support India’s net-zero goals and Australia’s clean energy role.
 Migration and          Expand skilled mobility pathways under the MATES Scheme as a blueprint for high-skill talent
 Mobility               exchange while simplifying the general visa process for students.
 Partnership            Australia should focus not just on exporting raw uranium but also on collaborative technology transfer,
 Beyond Uranium         specialised mining techniques, and research into new nuclear technologies.
India and Australia today are no longer distant Commonwealth partners but natural strategic allies shaping the Indo-Pacific
century. Anchored in shared democratic values, maritime interests, critical mineral cooperation, and technology partnerships,
the relationship has evolved from a commodity-driven engagement to a comprehensive strategic partnership.
pyq: The time has come for India and Japan to build a strong contemporary relationship, one involving a global and strategic partnership that will have a great significance for Asia and the world as a whole. Comment. (2019)
Economic ties between India and Japan, while growing in recent years, are still far below their potential. Elucidate the policy constraints which are inhibiting this growth. (2013)

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
7               India-New Zealand Relations

Relations between India and New Zealand have evolved from Commonwealth-era goodwill and migration links into a modern
Indo-Pacific partnership driven by trade diversification, strategic balancing, and human mobility. The partnership reflects
India’s outreach to smaller yet influential Pacific economies and New Zealand’s search for reliable economic and security partners
beyond traditional alliances.
Evolution of India–New Zealand Relations
              Phase                                                        Description
 Initial Diplomatic Phase            Establishment of diplomatic relations; cooperation mainly through Commonwealth
 (1952–1990)                         linkages, education exchanges and limited trade engagement.
 Engagement Expansion Phase          Post-liberalisation India increased economic outreach; trade, agriculture cooperation, and
 (1991–2010)                         people-to-people ties expanded gradually
 Strategic Partnership Building      High-level visits, defence dialogue, education cooperation and negotiations for a
 (2011–2019)                         Comprehensive Economic Cooperation Agreement (CECA) initiated.
 Indo-Pacific Convergence            Cooperation deepened in Indo-Pacific security, climate action, supply chains, and
 Phase (2020–2024)                   multilateral forums reflecting shared democratic values.
 FTA Negotiation Acceleration        India and New Zealand concluded negotiations for a Free Trade Agreement aimed at
 (2025)                              doubling bilateral trade and expanding investment cooperation.
 Economic Partnership                Formal signing of the India–New Zealand FTA, granting wide tariff reductions, duty-free
 Transformation (2026)               access for goods, investment commitments and enhanced mobility provisions

Significance of India–New Zealand Relations
          Aspect                           Description                                         Examples

 Partnership                  chain diversification through stronger      access for Indian exports and boosts agriculture and
                              economic engagement.                        services trade.

 Mobility                     human capital development and talent        quotas for Indian professionals in IT, engineering,
                              exchange.                                   and AYUSH sectors.

 Innovation                   ethics, and space-based remote sensing.     innovation hubs in agritech and clean technology.

 Sustainability               climate resilience, and sustainable         climate-smart agriculture initiatives.
 Cooperation                  farming systems
 Agriculture & Food           Collaboration in dairy tech, food           Joint research on climate-resilient livestock breeds
 Security                     processing, and sustainable agriculture     and soil microbiome improvement
                              practices.
 People-to-People Ties        The Indian diaspora strengthens cultural    New Zealand is home to a large diaspora of around
                              diplomacy and economic connectivity.        300,000 people originating from all over India.

Key Features of the India–New Zealand FTA (2026)
         Aspect                                                          Description
 Trade in Goods               100% duty-free access for all Indian exports to New Zealand, benefiting labour-intensive sectors
 (Exports)                    like textiles, leather, gems & jewellery, and engineering goods.
 Trade in Goods               India liberalised 70.03% of tariff lines (≈95% trade value); 30% eliminated immediately, and
 (Imports)                    35.6% phased out over 3–10 years.


Sensitive Sector           India protected 29.97% tariff lines, excluding dairy, key agricultural items, and sensitive
Protection                 domestic industries.

Commitment                 like agriculture, manufacturing, infrastructure & startups.

Professionals              for Indian professionals.

                           scholars) without caps.

Partnership                productivity and incomes.
AYUSH & Traditional        Cooperation on Ayurveda, Yoga, and wellness services; facilitates registration of Indian
Knowledge                  Geographical Indications.
Manufacturing              Duty-free import of industrial inputs like coking coal, wooden logs, and metal scrap to reduce
Support                    manufacturing costs.
Government                 Greater transparency in public tenders while protecting MSME-reserved sectors and defence
Procurement                procurement.
Sanitary and               The Joint Technical Committee harmonises standards, easing exports such as Indian mangoes and
Phytosanitary standards    pomegranates.

Challenges in India–New Zealand Relations
       Aspect                           Description                                            Examples
Trade Imbalance &         Bilateral trade remains below potential due     Trade is dominated by dairy, education, and
Limited Trade             to limited diversification.                     agricultural products (70-80%) with limited
Volume                                                                    diversification.

Distance &                trade costs and limits business                 direct flights affects trade expansion.
Connectivity              engagement.
Limited Strategic         The relationship remains primarily economic     Defence and security cooperation is still evolving
Convergence               and people-centric rather than strategic        compared to the Indo-Pacific QUAD partnership.
Geopolitical              New Zealand’s participation in the Five         Intelligence consultations involving partners like
Trust Gap                 Eyes network occasionally raises security       Canada created diplomatic concerns.
                          sensitivities for India.
