                          affect Indian students.                         have forced many Indian students into substandard
                                                                          housing.
Migration & Mobility      Visa norms, labour mobility regulations,        Indian skilled workers face licensing and
Issues                    and recognition of qualifications affect        accreditation barriers in certain sectors such as
                          professional exchanges.                         healthcare.

Way Forward

       Aspect                                                           Description
Institutional              Transitioning the FTA from a legal document to a functional trade pipeline by setting up Joint
Implementation             Monitoring Committees.
Strategic Dialogue         Institutionalise regular strategic and defence dialogues to deepen political trust and security
                           cooperation in the Indo-Pacific.
Education & Mobility       Expand student mobility, research collaboration, skill recognition frameworks, and innovation
                           exchanges between universities.
Defense & Maritime         Moving from port calls to Deep Security Interoperability in the Indo-Pacific. Enhance maritime
Security                   cooperation, regional connectivity, and rules-based order initiatives in the Indo-Pacific region.


 Technology &               Promote joint work in AI, agri-tech, fintech, climate tech, and digital governance through startup
 Collaboration              and innovation partnerships.

 Cooperation                practices & agri-value chain integration
 Security Confidence        Improve transparency and intelligence dialogue to address concerns arising from New Zealand’s
 Building                   participation in the Five Eyes framework.
India–New Zealand relations are transitioning from people-centric and education-driven ties to a comprehensive economic
and Indo-Pacific partnership anchored in trade, technology, and sustainable development. The 2026 FTA marks a structural
shift, positioning both countries as complementary partners in resilient supply chains, agri-innovation, and mobility
cooperation.

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
8               India - France Relations

India-France ties have evolved into a Special Global Strategic Partnership (2026) based on shared values of democracy, sovereignty,
multilateralism, and strategic autonomy. The relationship has expanded from defence and nuclear cooperation to climate action,
Indo-Pacific collaboration, space, digital ties, and people-to-people links, with both countries working to strengthen a rules-based
global order.


Areas of Cooperation in India-France Relations
      Area                             Description                                            Key Examples
 Civil Nuclear       France has supported India’s civil nuclear             Jaitapur Nuclear Power Project (world’s largest
 & Space             programme since 2008 & is a key partner in space       planned plant); joint ISRO-CNES missions

                     global climate platforms.                              (2023)

                     Intelligence (AI), and secure cyberspace               in Bengaluru, etc.

                     buildings, industry, and transport.

   Dimension                       Significance for India                                         Examples

                       international issues.                                   terrorism, etc.

 Partner               where both nations seek a stable, rule-based order      Indo-Pacific (La Réunion, New Caledonia), with
                       for security and trade.                                 1.6 million citizens & 93% of its EEZ there.
 Defence               Since 1998, France has been a key defence partner,      Rafale jet deal; Joint exercises: Varuna (Navy),
                       supporting trade, joint exercises, counter-terrorism,   Garuda (Air Force), Shakti (Army); India accounts
                       and Indo-Pacific security.                              for a major chunk of France’s arms exports.
 Indian                Cultural exchanges, education cooperation, and a        Expansion of student mobility targets to 30,000
 Diaspora              growing Indian diaspora deepen long-term societal       Indian students in France by 2030
                       ties and soft power engagement.
 Minilateral           France acts as a key European strategic partner,        India–France–UAE Trilateral, India–France–
 Partnerships          helping India expand flexible minilateral diplomacy     Australia Trilateral, and coordination in the
                       beyond traditional alliances.                           Western Indian Ocean and West Asia.
 Security              Cooperation enhances counter-terrorism coordination,    White Shipping Agreement, joint anti-terror
                       maritime domain awareness, intelligence sharing, and    cooperation, and defence industrial collaboration,
                       defence industrial resilience.                          including submarine and aerospace projects.

                       through a roadmap for ocean governance and              (India) and IFREMER (France) are partnering
                       maritime sustainability.                                under India's Deep Ocean Mission.

Horizon 2047 Agreement
The Horizon 2047 Roadmap is a long-term strategic partnership blueprint adopted during the 2023 Bastille Day visit of the
Indian Prime Minister to France. It sets a 25-year vision to guide India–France relations until 2047, marking 100 years of India’s
independence.


Challenges in the India-France Relationship
   Challenge Area                                 Description                                         Examples
 Trade and Investment        Despite growing trade, economic engagement remains         Bilateral trade crossed over $15 bn in
                             below potential due to regulatory differences and slow     2024-25 but remains modest compared
                             market-access negotiations.                                to India-EU trade (~$135+ bn).
 Delays in Civil             There are significant, long-standing delays in the civil   The Jaitapur Nuclear Power Project

                                                                                        renewed liability reform discussions.

 on Global Conflicts         France’s strong pro-Ukraine position, affecting            resolutions; France condemned Russia’s
                             cooperation on global platforms.                           actions.
 Weak Intellectual           French companies seek stronger enforcement certainty       High-tech and pharma firms remain

                             governance frameworks in India.                            licensing and the speed of patent
                                                                                        enforcement.

Way Forward
     Priority Area                                                      Description

                            renewables.

                            Partnership.

 Mobility                   and cultural ties.

 Sustainability             to advance renewable energy, resilience, and global climate action.
India and France represent a trusted strategic partnership anchored in strategic autonomy, defence cooperation, and maritime
security. Guided by shared values of a rules-based international order and multipolarity, the partnership is expanding into clean
energy, digital technologies, and climate action. Together, they act as stabilising forces in the Indo-Pacific, blending pragmatism
with long-term global responsibility.

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
9              India - Canada Relations

India–Canada relations, established in 1947, are based on shared democratic values, Commonwealth ties, and a 1.8 million-strong
diaspora. While early cooperation under the Colombo Plan was strong, differences over nuclear policy and Khalistan-related
concerns strained ties, reaching a low after the 2023 crisis. Since 2026, both sides have been pursuing a “strategic reset,” focusing
on energy (uranium, LNG), critical minerals, clean tech, education, and trade growth by 2030.


Areas of Cooperation in India-Canada Relations
         Area                                   Description                                            Example
 Bilateral                 Strengthening economic ties via the proposed                Both recently signed the Terms of
 Trade                     Comprehensive Economic Partnership                          Reference for the Comprehensive
                           Agreement (CEPA).                                           Economic Partnership Agreement.
 Science &                 Joint initiatives in clean tech, health, AI, space, and     IC-IMPACTS programme; ISRO–CSA
 Technology                smart infrastructure under bilateral platforms.             space research collaboration.
 Education                 Canada is a top destination for Indian students; higher     40% of Canada’s international students
                           education builds long-term people-to-people ties.           are of Indian origin.

 Energy                    security and climate goals.

 Collaboration             systems, and joint R&D in defence production.               Corporation MoU, renewed in 2021.

 Consular Dialogue         student safety, and manage irregular migration.             and 2022 to address bilateral concerns.

                          through Canadian pension funds.                     (CPPIB) has invested over US$15 billion in India.

                          strengthens India’s soft power in Canada.

Normative             on human rights, gender equity, pluralism,          Partnership for Artificial Intelligence (GPAI),
Alignment             and the rule of law on global platforms.            promoting ethical AI.
Global                Both seek reform of multilateral institutions       Canada supported India’s stance during the G20

Reform                equitable global representation.

                      peaceful nuclear cooperation.                       to India (2027–2035)

                      cooperation.                                        goals.

Cooperation           supply chains, which is vital for EVs and           exploration, processing, and stockpiling initiatives.
                      clean technologies.
Technology &          Expansion in AI, quantum tech, digital public       Finalized the Australia - Canada -India Technology
Innovation            infrastructure, startups and research mobility.     & Innovation trilateral to co-develop 6G and
Partnership                                                               sovereign AI systems.

Challenges in India-Canada Relations
  Challenge                           Description                                              Example

 Breakdown          severely strained bilateral relations and reduced     withdrawal of 41 Canadian diplomats and marked

                                                                          decades.

 Geopolitical       developmental diplomacy in Southeast Asia and         Act East interests, creating rivalry for influence in
 Divergence         Africa, leading to friction in multilateral forums.   the same maritime zones.

Khalistani Issue Between India and Canada Relations
     Aspect                             Description                                             Example
Threat to India’s    Khalistani activities promote separatism in            Organisations like SFJ and Khalistan Liberation
Sovereignty          Punjab, which India classifies as extremism and        Force use social media to spread propaganda and
                     terrorism.                                             misinformation in Punjab.
Free Speech vs       Canada frames Khalistani activities under              Canada allowed Khalistan “referendums” by
National Security    freedom of expression, while India argues that         groups like Sikhs for Justice (SFJ) as free speech
Debate               advocacy crosses into support for terrorism.           under India’s objection.
Diplomatic Safety    Attacks and threats against Indian consulates and      SFJ announced a "siege" on the Indian consulate
                     diplomats by extremists are a key security concern     in Vancouver in September 2025.
                     for India.

                     supported their proxies in India to carry out          officially listed Babbar Khalsa International as a
                     attacks in India through money laundering.             group receiving financial support.
Diplomatic           The killing of a Khalistani leader escalated           Canada accused India of the killing of Hardeep
tensions             tensions, leading to a serious diplomatic              Singh Nijjar, leading to reciprocal diplomatic
                     confrontation and mutual allegations.                  reprisals and tensions.


Way Forward
         Measure                                                         Description
 Strengthen Political           Restore high-level diplomatic engagement through regular ministerial dialogue, confidence-
 Relations                      building measures, and institutional communication channels to rebuild trust after recent
                                tensions.
 Address Mutual                 Engage in open and lawful dialogue at the National Security Advisor (NSA) level on
 Security                       Khalistan and other mutual security issues, ensuring mutual respect for sovereignty, legal
 Concerns                       frameworks, and concerns of the diaspora.
 Expedite                       Though the terms of reference for the Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement (CEPA)
 Trade                          have been signed, both nations should aggressively pursue its finalisation to improve trade ties.
 Enhance Strategic              Strengthen collaboration in counterterrorism, cyber-security, and promote a free, open, and
 Cooperation                    rules-based Indo-Pacific to align strategic interests.
 Promote Environmental          Deepen cooperation in renewable energy, climate change mitigation, and technology transfer
 Collaboration                  to meet shared sustainability goals.
India–Canada ties have entered a strategic reset, shifting from political tensions to a pragmatic, economy-driven partnership. Focus
on energy, critical minerals, and the 2026 uranium pact, along with revived CEPA talks, reflects deepening cooperation, while
remaining differences are managed through quiet diplomacy to sustain stable, forward-looking relations.

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
10                India - Israel Relations

India–Israel relations have transformed into a Special Strategic Partnership for Peace, Innovation & Prosperity" (2026),
anchored in strategic autonomy, shared democratic values, and security convergence. Spanning defence, innovation,
agriculture, critical technologies, and geoeconomic cooperation, the partnership reflects India’s pragmatic West Asia policy
and its pursuit of multi-alignment in a rapidly evolving regional order.

                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    “India’s relations with Israel have, of late, acquired a depth and diversity, which cannot
           be rolled back. " Discuss. (2018)


Area of Cooperation in India-Israel Relations
       Area                               Description                                                  Examples
 Strategic           Strategic ties are based on shared values, counter-terror        India’s 2017 visit was the first by an Indian
 Cooperation         cooperation, and strong shared interests in West Asian           PM, and Israel supported India in UN
                     stability.                                                       forums on terrorism.
 Economic            Diamonds, chemicals, electronics, and high-tech goods            In FY 2023-24 and FY 2024-25, the bilateral
 Engagement          drive bilateral trade. Talks on an India–Israel FTA are          trade (excluding defence) was US$ 6.53
                     ongoing. Trade reflects commercial depth beyond                  billion and US$3.75 billion, respectively
                     defence.

 Technology          dryland agriculture through tech transfer and capacity           Agriculture (IINCA).
                     building.
 Science &           Growing ties in cybersecurity, AI, space, and clean              Partnerships exist between CERT-In (India)
 Technology          energy support startup ecosystems accelerated by the             and INCD (Israel) to protect critical digital
                     India-Israel Industrial R&D and Innovation Fund                  infrastructure, including a Joint Cyber Centre
                     (I4F)                                                            of Excellence.

Significance of India–Israel Relations
  Dimension                      Significance for India                                              Example

                    rapidly.                                                      billion (FY 2022-23, excluding defence).

                      and intelligence sharing.

 & Startup            innovation and entrepreneurship ecosystem.                  with Israeli R&D in semiconductors and quantum
                                                                                  tech.

                      states, Iran.                                               footprint.

 and Exports          connecting the Gulf to Europe under IMEC.                   (40% faster) and costs (30% lower) & logistical
                                                                                  security.

                      goods for the future.                                       technology, AI, and security.
 Economic             Transition to a comprehensive trade relationship that       UPI in Israel: MoU signed to enable Unified
 Depth                protects labour and boosts services.                        Payments Interface for Indian workers and
                                                                                  travellers.

Israel-Palestine Conflict
The Israel–Palestine conflict is a long-standing dispute over land, sovereignty, and national identity, mainly concerning territories
like the West Bank and Gaza. Israel seeks security and recognition as a state, while Palestinians demand an independent homeland.
Periodic violence keeps the conflict as one of the most complex geopolitical issues in the Middle East.

India's Stand in the Israel-Palestine Conflict

 Two-State Solution       India supports the peaceful coexistence of Israel      India regularly backs UN resolutions supporting
                          and Palestine through negotiations and                 an independent Palestinian state alongside Israel.
                          diplomacy

                          security and technology while maintaining              India-Israel relations to a Special Strategic
                          separate ties with Palestine.                          Partnership in Jerusalem (2026)

 Chains                wartime needs can delay supplies.                        timelines tied to Israel’s domestic stock levels

 Security              political and logistical challenge for India.            citizens from Israel.
 Strategic             Escalations make the de-hyphenation policy harder        India abstained from human rights probes while
 Autonomy              to sustain, facing pressure from the Global South (to    voting for a two-state solution in the 2026 Delhi
                       condemn Israel) and the West (to support it).            Declaration.


Key Challenges in India-Israel Relations
  Challenge Area                           Description                                             Example

                           Make in India goals in defence.

 Defence Imports           raises concerns about strategic autonomy            Barak-8 air defence system, Heron drones, and
                           and supply vulnerability during conflicts.          Harop loitering munition.
 Labour Safety             Sending large numbers of Indian workers to a        Escalation risks affecting Indian workers and
 & Risk                    conflict-prone zone creates a massive               shipping routes in West Asia during the Gaza
                           domestic and security liability.                    conflict tensions (2023–24).
 The China                 China remains Israel’s largest trading partner      India remains cautious about dual-use tech
 Factor                    in Asia. High Chinese investment in Israeli         developed in Israel that might have Chinese
                           tech is a security concern for India.               investment or hardware components.

5. Way Forward
        Measure                                                             Description
 Technology Institutions       Moving beyond MOUs to permanent joint institutions for AI, Quantum, and Semiconductors.
 Transition in Defense         Israel needs to facilitate technology transfer and expand joint manufacturing of missile systems,
                               drones, and air-defence technologies developed with Israeli firms.
 Technology and                Strengthen projects under the India–Israel Industrial R&D and Technological Innovation
 Innovation                    Fund (I4F) supporting joint startups.
 Finalise Economic             Concluding the Free Trade Agreement (FTA) to diversify trade beyond diamonds and
 Ties                          chemicals.
 Operationalize                Integrating Israeli logistics with Indian financial hubs to streamline the India-Middle East-
 Connectivity                  Europe corridor.
 Labour & Skill Mobility       Creating a safe, legal, and high-skill corridor for Indian professionals.
 Energy & Climate              Expand cooperation in green hydrogen, solar innovation, and climate-smart infrastructure.
 Cooperation
 People-to-People &            Promote student exchange, tourism, and research partnerships to build long-term societal ties.
 Academic Links
India–Israel ties should increasingly move from a buyer–seller relationship to co-development, co-production, and
innovation-led collaboration under the spirit of Atmanirbhar Bharat. At the same time, India must uphold its policy of
strategic autonomy, supporting Israel's security alongside a viable two-state solution for lasting peace.

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
11                 India - Central Asia Relations

India-Central Asia relations are rooted in shared civilisational ties and viewed as an “Extended Neighbourhood”. Strengthened by
the Connect Central Asia Policy (2012), they focus on connectivity, energy security, and counter-terrorism (especially Afghanistan).
India engages Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Kyrgyzstan, and Tajikistan through INSTC and Chabahar, supported by the
India–Central Asia Dialogue (2019) and Ashgabat Agreement (2018), to promote regional stability and Eurasian integration
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     Critically analyse India's evolving diplomatic, economic and strategic relations with the
             Central Asian Republics (CARs), highlighting their increasing significance in regional
             and global geopolitics. (2024)
      2.     A number of outside powers have entrenched themselves in Central Asia, which is a
             zone of interest to India. Discuss the implications, in this context, of India’s joining the
             Ashgabat Agreement, 2018. (2018)


Areas of Cooperation between India and Central Asia
           Area                                  Description                                            Example
 Energy                   Cooperation in hydrocarbons, nuclear fuel, and energy           TAPI pipeline for gas imports from
                          connectivity for secure supply                                  Turkmenistan

 Security                 sharing, tackling extremism, and drug trafficking.              (Dustlik) & Kyrgyzstan (Khanjar)
 Multilateral             Cooperation via regional platforms for security and             Engagement through SCO
 Engagement               economic integration
 Cultural                 Cooperation focuses on shared civilisational ties and           Buddhist tourism and cultural
 Linkages                 people-to-people exchanges.                                     exchanges

 Technology               infrastructure.                                                 telemedicine initiatives

                         Europe                                                      Europe.

terrorism                trafficking from Afghanistan to India                       mechanism
Countering               India’s engagement with Central Asia strengthens its        India DPI (Aadhaar, UPI) and training in IT,
China                    strategic presence amid China’s rising influence.           pharma, and healthcare reduce reliance on
                                                                                     Chinese tech.
Trade                    Central Asia offers new markets for Indian pharma,          Indian pharma products are a major part of the
Opportunities            IT, textiles, and agriculture exports.                      trade basket with Central Asian countries.

                     balance, thus limiting India’s influence.                    Tajikistan

Constraints          Pakistan factor                                              strengthens China’s reach
Diplomatic           Central Asian states balance China, Russia, and India,       Kazakhstan prefers Russian security and
Balance              often prioritising China for economic gains and              Chinese investment more while working with
                     Russia for security.                                         India on digital infrastructure and the INSTC.

Challenges between India and Central Asia
     Aspect                            Description                                                Example
Low Trade              India’s trade with the region is low compared        India-Central Asia trade is ~$1.7-2 billion. In
                       to other trade partners, often restricted by the     contrast, China-Central Asia trade has reached a
                       landlocked nature of the region.                     record $106.3 billion in 2025.

Connectivity           costs and limits trade                               Chabahar route

                       sanctions and instability
Financial              Weak banking links and sanctions hinder              Indian exporters trading with Uzbekistan frequently
Constraints            trade                                                rely on intermediary banks in the UAE due to the
                                                                            absence of robust direct banking channels.


Way Forward
          Aspect                                                        Description
 Increasing                   Boosting Central Asia trade secures energy (oil, gas, and uranium); diversifies imports; expands
 Trade                        mineral access; and deepens ties in renewables and green hydrogen.
 Connectivity                 Accelerate multimodal connectivity projects to overcome geographical barriers and ensure
 Enhancement                  reliable access to Central Asian markets.
 Counter-Terror               Deepen intelligence sharing and regional security engagement to address extremism and
 Cooperation                  instability.
 Institutional                Regular high-level summits and structured dialogue mechanisms to sustain momentum through
 Engagement                   India–Central Asia Summit follow-ups and ministerial dialogues.
 Digital & Development        Expand digital public infrastructure, fintech cooperation, telemedicine, and e-governance
 Cooperation                  partnerships like Sharing India Stack–type digital governance models
 Multilateral &               Strengthening coordination with regional groupings while maintaining strategic autonomy by
 Regional Balancing           working with Eurasian partners without entering geopolitical blocs.
India–Central Asia relations are rooted in civilisational connectivity but increasingly driven by strategic, energy, and security
imperatives. As geopolitical competition intensifies in Eurasia, deeper engagement through connectivity corridors, economic
partnerships, and multilateral platforms like the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation becomes crucial. Strengthening this partnership
will help India enhance continental outreach, balance regional powers, and secure long-term strategic autonomy in Eurasia.
pyq: Critically analyse India's evolving diplomatic, economic and strategic relations with the Central Asian Republics (CARs), highlighting their increasing significance in regional and global geopolitics. (2024)

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
12                 India-Africa Relations

The relationship between India and Africa is rooted in shared historical experiences of colonialism, South–South cooperation,
and common developmental aspirations. From early solidarity during anti-colonial struggles to today’s expanding engagement
in trade, capacity building, technology, and security, India–Africa relations have evolved into a comprehensive strategic
partnership.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    India-Africa digital partnership is achieving mutual respect, co-development and long-
           term institutional partnerships. Elaborate (2025)
     2.    “If the last few decades were of Asia’s growth story, the next few are expected to be of
           Africa’s.” In the light of this statement, examine India’s influence in Africa in recent
           years. (2021)
     3.    Increasing interest of India in Africa has its pro and cons. Critically examine. (2015)

        Aspect                                                         Description

India–Africa relations can be seen as a bridge connecting the aspirations of the Global South, where cooperation is guided by
capacity building rather than conditionality. As the world moves toward multipolarity, this partnership has the potential to emerge
as a model of equitable and people-centric international cooperation shaping a more inclusive global order.

chapters.part: Bilateral Groupings and Agreements Involving India and / or Affecting India’s Interests
chapters.text:
13                 India-Latin America Relations

Historically separated by geography but united by similar developmental challenges, India and Latin American nations increasingly
cooperate in energy security, pharmaceuticals, agriculture, digital technology, and multilateral forums. The region is crucial
for India’s access to critical minerals, food security partnerships, and diversification of economic and strategic engagements beyond
traditional partners.


Significance of India-Latin America Relations
      Aspect                             Description                                              Example
 Trade Growth           Latin America is a growing trade partner of         Trade between India and Latin America is expected
                        India, with a target of $100 bn by 2030.            to double to Rs. 9.47 lakh crore (US$ 100 billion)
                                                                            by 2030 from the current Rs. 4.74 lakh crore
                                                                            (US$ 50 billion) .
 Investment Hub         Latin America is a Key base for Indian              Investments ~$20 bn with investors like Tata,
                        manufacturing and services                          ONGC Videsh Ltd., etc.
 Global South           Latin America supports India’s role in a            Joint advocacy in G20, BRICS, IBSA
 Partnership            multipolar world.
 Strategic              India’s strategic autonomy aligns with Latin        Both view the Russia–Ukraine war as regional,
 Autonomy               America’s non alignment, resisting major            resisting sanctions and focusing on price stability.
                        powers’ pressure.
 Food Security          LAC is now India’s main alternative source for      After the 2022 Ukraine war, India’s edible oil
                        edible oil after Black Sea disruptions.             imports from LAC rose from $2.4B to $5.6B.
 Critical Minerals      Latin America’s Lithium Triangle supports           KABIL secured clearance to explore lithium brine

India–Latin America relations resemble a bridge across oceans connecting resources with technology, markets with innovation,
and shared aspirations with global influence. As the international order becomes increasingly multipolar, this partnership is poised
to emerge as a key pillar of diversified diplomacy, sustainable growth, and South–South cooperation in the 21st century.

         1               G20

The G20 is the premier forum for international economic cooperation, shaping global governance on key economic issues. Established
in 1999 after the Asian financial crisis and elevated to a leaders’ level in 2008 during the global financial crisis, it is the central
platform for global economic coordination. Its agenda now spans trade, sustainable development, climate, and health. India’s
Presidency (Dec 2022–Nov 2023) was significant, including the African Union as a permanent member.

Introduction
    Aspect                                                             Details
 About G20         y The Group of Twenty (G20) is an international forum comprising 19 countries, the European Union
                     (EU) and the African Union (AU), representing major economies to address global economic and social
                     challenges.
 Significance      y Represents ~85% of global GDP, 75% of global trade, and 60% of the world’s population, making it
                     a pivotal platform for global governance.
                   y India’s leadership in 2023 pushed for the Global South.
 Purpose           y Promotes global economic stability, sustainable development, and cooperation on issues like poverty,
                     climate change, and geopolitical conflicts.
 G20 Summit        y The 18th G20 Summit was held in India (2023). The substantive centrepiece of the G20 Summit was the
                     New Delhi G20 Leaders’ Declaration (NDLD).
                   y The 2026 G20 Leaders’ Summit will be hosted by the United States in Miami, Florida.

Key Takeaways of the 20th G20 Summit
The 20th G20 Summit was a historic milestone, held for the first time on the African continent in Johannesburg, South Africa
(November 22–23, 2025). Under the theme "Solidarity, Equality, Sustainability," and philosophy of Ubuntu the summit concluded
the four-year "Global South Cycle" of presidencies (Indonesia, India, Brazil, and South Africa).

The G20 provides India a pivotal platform to act as a bridge between the Global North and Global South, advancing inclusive
growth, climate justice, and reform of global governance institutions. For India, the G20 is not merely a forum for economic
coordination but a strategic instrument to promote multilateralism, development equity, and a balanced multipolar world order
aligned with its vision of “Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam.”

2             SAARC

The South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC) is the principal regional organisation in South Asia,
established on 8 December 1985 to foster economic integration, collective self-reliance, and socio-cultural cooperation
among its member states. Despite its significant institutional framework, its potential has been constrained by geopolitical
rivalries and bilateral tensions, particularly between India and Pakistan.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    “Increasing cross-border terrorist attacks in India and growing interference in the
           internal affairs of several member-states by Pakistan are not conducive for the future
           of SAARC (South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation).” Explain with suitable
           examples. (2016)

                            with it.                                          stance.

Asia’s collective potential. For India, a more integrated neighbourhood aligns with its Neighbourhood First Policy, strengthens
its aspiration of becoming a net security provider and leading power, and advances the vision of “Viksit Bharat 2047”
through regional peace and shared prosperity.
pyq: “Increasing cross-border terrorist attacks in India and growing interference in the internal affairs of several member-states by Pakistan are not conducive for the future of SAARC (South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation).” Explain with suitable examples. (2016)

chapters.text:
3            BRICS

BRICS, coined by Jim O’Neill in 2001, unites Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa, and new members Egypt, Ethiopia, Iran,
the UAE, and Indonesia to foster cooperation of emerging economies. It represents 49.5% of the world population, 40% of world
GDP and 26% global trade share. BRICS amplifies India’s geopolitical influence, secures developmental funding, and promotes
equitable global governance through sustainable multilateral frameworks.
                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    India has recently signed to become a founding member of New Development Bank
           (NDB) and also the Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB). How will the role of
           the two banks be different? Discuss the Strategic significance of these two Banks for
           India. (2014)

Evolution of BRICS
    Aspect                              Description                                              Example
 Genesis            Formalised in 2006 during the G8 Outreach Summit in        The first BRIC Summit was held in
                    St. Petersburg, Russia.                                    Yekaterinburg, Russia, 2009.
 Expansion to       South Africa joined in 2010, transforming BRIC into        South Africa’s inclusion was formalised at the
 BRICS              BRICS, enhancing African representation.                   3rd BRICS Summit in Sanya, China, 2011
 Recent             - 2024 with Egypt, Ethiopia, Iran, and the UAE;            Indonesia’s entry as a full member at the 17th
 Expansion          - Indonesia joined in January 2025.                        Summit, Rio de Janeiro, 2025

Structure of BRICS
      Aspect                                                          Description
 Governance            Operates through annual rotating chairships, summits, and ministerial meetings, lacking a permanent
                       secretariat.
 Mechanisms            Includes Leaders’ Summits, ministerial meetings (foreign ministers and NSAs), and technical groups for
                       trade, health, and technology. Business engagement via the BRICS Business Council & Women
                       Business Alliance.
 Membership            Comprises 11 member states and 10 partner countries, balancing inclusivity with decision-making
                       efficiency.

Brazil’s 2025 Presidency: Key Themes and Objectives
Brazil assumed the BRICS Chair in January 2025 under the theme “Strengthening Global South Cooperation for Inclusive and
Sustainable Governance", culminating in the Rio Summit (July 2025).

          Theme                             Objective                                           Details
 Global South               Strengthen ties among developing nations        UNSC & WTO reforms to enhance Global South
 Cooperation                for a multipolar world.                         representation

 Financial Reforms          autonomy.                                       reduce US dollar reliance
 Global Health              Enhance health collaboration for sustainable    Partnership for Elimination of Socially Determined
 Cooperation                development.                                    Diseases
 Climate                    Mobilise climate finance and sustainable        Endorsed the BRICS Carbon Markets Partnership
 Change                     infrastructure.                                 and the Tropical Forest Forever Fund
 AI Governance              Develop inclusive AI frameworks.                Adopted BRICS Leaders’ Declaration on Global
                                                                            AI Governance
 Institutional              Improve BRICS cohesion and decision-            Proposed a permanent secretariat for streamlined
 Development                making.                                         operations

    Dimension                              Description                                           Example
Global South Voice        BRICS amplifies the Global South's voice in          Push for UNSC reforms (17th 2025 Summit)
                          UNSC, IMF, and WTO reforms

                          GDP, trade & population.                             population, surpassing G7 GDP share

                          BRICS central to energy governance                   of global crude oil output
Development               Alternative financing via NDB                        $490 million NDB loan for Madhya Pradesh
Cooperation                                                                    highways Project
Parallel Platform         Alternative to Western-led institutions such as      BRICS+ expands outreach to Global South
                          the G7.                                              partners.
De - dollarisation        Promotes local-currency trade and payment            India–Russia rupee–ruble trade
                          systems to reduce dollar dependence.
Counter-                  Coordinates action against terrorism, extremism,     BRICS statements stress countering terror
Terrorism                 financing, and cyber threats.                        financing and cross-border terrorism
Multipolar                BRICS signals a shift from Western unipolarity       BRICS agreed on a human-centric AI
Order                     to a multipolar order.                               framework to curb Western tech dominance.

Key Initiatives of BRICS
        Initiative                                                        Description
New Development Bank            Established in 2015 to finance infrastructure and sustainable development projects in
(NDB)                           emerging economies, providing an alternative to Western-led financial institutions.

Arrangement (CRA)               facing balance-of-payments crises.

                                transactions in local currencies.
BRICS Startup                   A centralised digital repository and engagement platform to link startups, investors, and
Knowledge Hub                   incubators across member states.

Cooperation Platform            renewable energy tech.

Framework                       South data sovereignty.

Challenges Facing BRICS
     Challenge                              Description                                          Example
Geopolitical Rivalries     Internal tensions weaken consensus                 India–China border tensions
Low Intra-BRICS            Limited trade due to distance, similar exports     India’s $209 billion trade deficit with BRICS in
Trade                      and China-centric trade flows.                     2024
Limited Global             BRICS institutions have a weaker financial         NDB’s funding lags behind the World Bank's.
Influence                  capacity than their Western counterparts
De-dollarisation Limits    Currency volatility and external pressure          US pushback on alternative currency plans
                           hinder progress
Institutional Weakness     No permanent secretariat slows coordination        The BRICS credit rating plan stalled due to low
                                                                              consensus

                           order                                              expansion
Chinese Dominance          China’s economic size creates an imbalance         China leads in GDP and agenda-setting
                           within BRICS


India’s Strategic Role in BRICS
      Aspect                                     Role                                               Example

                         checks China’s dominance

 Leadership              cooperation                                               reforms in finance

 Engagement

 Benefits                                                                          and energy sectors.
 Security                Pushes counter-terrorism cooperation with India’s         Condemnation of the Pahalgam attack at the
 Advocacy                priorities.                                               17th Summit, 2025
 Climate                 Promotes renewables and climate equity led by the         Solar cooperation through ISA and green
 Leadership              Global South.                                             transition push

Comparison: NDB vs. AIIB
         Aspect                           New Development                                    Asian Infrastructure
                                            Bank (NDB)                                     Investment Bank (AIIB)
 Established By             BRICS countries (2014).                                China-led (2013).
 Membership                 Five founding BRICS nations and new members            100+ members (India is the 2nd largest
                            Bangladesh, UAE, Egypt, and Algeria                    shareholder).
 Voting Power               Equal among members, ensuring balanced                 Based on shareholding, China holds the
                            influence.                                             highest share.
 Focus                      Infrastructure & sustainable development in            Infrastructure, primarily in Asia.
                            emerging economies.
 Example Projects           Renewable energy, smart grid (MP solar project).       Mumbai Metro and Andhra Pradesh road
 (India)                                                                           development.

Way Forward
         Strategy                                                         Action Plan
 Institutional Reforms          Establish a permanent secretariat and weighted voting for economic decisions to enhance
                                efficiency.
 Financial Integration          Expand NDB lending, promote the BRICS+ Free Trade Agreement, and develop alternative
                                SWIFT systems.
 Geopolitical Cooperation       Strengthen counter-terrorism and conflict resolution platforms for unified stances.
 Innovation Partnerships        Form a BRICS+ Digital Alliance for AI, semiconductors, and green tech R&D.
 Cultural Exchange              Promote the BRICS+ University Network and visa-free tourism to foster people-to-people ties.
 Addressing West’s              Ensure BRICS remains a reform-oriented platform rather than an anti-West bloc by promoting
 Concerns                       transparency, rules-based cooperation, and inclusive multilateralism
BRICS is a key pillar of a multipolar world, advancing Global South aspirations for equitable global governance. As India assumes
the 2026 presidency after Brazil's 2025 leadership, it can strengthen BRICS through reforms, the New Development Bank, and
BRICS Pay to promote sustainable development and balanced global cooperation.
pyq: India has recently signed to become a founding member of New Development Bank (NDB) and also the Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB). How will the role of the two banks be different? Discuss the Strategic significance of these two Banks for India. (2014)

4            Shanghai Cooperation
                        Organisation (SCO)
The Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO), established in 2001, fosters political, economic, and security cooperation across
Eurasia. With ten members, including India (since 2017), it aims to counter terrorism, enhance trade, and promote cultural ties. For
India, SCO is a gateway to Central Asia, amplifying geopolitical influence. Despite challenges like China’s dominance, India’s
engagement strengthens regional stability and global multilateralism.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     ‘Virus of Conflict ' is affecting the functioning of the SCO. In light of the above statement,
             point out the role of India in mitigating problems. (2023)
      2.     Critically examine the aims and objectives of SCO. What importance does it hold for
             India? (2021)

Evolution and Structure
     Aspect                                                             Details
 Genesis              y Founded in 2001 by China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan as a successor
                        to the Shanghai Five (1996) to enhance regional security and cooperation.
                      y The SCO Charter, signed in 2002, came into force in 2003, formalising its objectives.
 Expansion            y Expanded to include India and Pakistan in 2017, Iran in 2023, and Belarus in 2024, totalling 10 members.
                      y Observer states and dialogue partners further broaden their reach.
 Structure            y Governed by the Heads of State Council (HSC), with permanent bodies like the Secretariat in Beijing
                        and the Regional Anti-Terrorist Structure (RATS) in Tashkent.
                      y Operates through annual summits and ministerial meetings.

                                                                            2025-27.

 Order                order that respects sovereignty and avoids            multipolar world order.
                      confrontation with other regions.

 Stability            beyond through joint initiatives and dialogue.        2005, facilitates regional cooperation with Kabul.
 Deepen               Enhances cooperation in trade, transport,             India’s SCO Startup Forum in 2021 fostered
 Engagements          tourism, culture, education, and technology to        innovation and entrepreneurship.
                      promote regional integration.

Significance for India

                       Asia through the Connect Central Asia            at the 25th SCO Heads of State Council Meeting

                       and natural gas.                                 framework to secure Turkmen gas for India.

                      sharing.                                             financiers, and perpetrators of the Pahalgam terror attack.

                      connectivity and trade access.                       Declaration, 2022, linking India to Eurasia.

                        building challenging.

                                                                           Mohammed

Perception              limits its global credibility.                     narrative that the SCO is an alliance targeting the West,
                                                                           referred to as an "axis of upheaval"

                        the Regional Anti-Terrorist Structure.             clashes.

     Role                              Details                                                   Examples
Conflict              India leverages non-aligned diplomacy to           India facilitated India-China LAC talks during the 2024
Resolution            mediate disputes, promoting dialogue.              Summit, easing border tensions.

                      economic ties.                                   Indian Ocean and global markets.

                      SCO framework.                                   International Terrorism (CCIT) within SCO

 Influence and        influence, promoting a balanced,                 transparency, opposing BRI.

 Inclusivity

SCO’s Role in Global Multilateralism
     Aspect                            Details                                                 Examples
 Geopolitical        Covers a vast Eurasian space, giving the           Covers 80% of Eurasia, 42% of the global population,
 Reach               SCO significant influence in shaping               and ~25% of GDP, amplifying its global influence.
                     regional and global affairs.
 Security Role       Provides a platform for addressing regional        At the 2025 SCO Defence Ministers' Meeting, member
                     security challenges, particularly terrorism,       states emphasized coordinated efforts against terrorism
                     extremism, and instability in Afghanistan          and the spillover effects of instability from Afghanistan.
 Alternative         Offers a non-Western platform for                  Belarus became a full SCO member in 2024, expanding
 Forum               cooperation, reflecting the aspirations of a       the grouping's geopolitical footprint.
                     multipolar world order.

Measures to Strengthen SCO
     Measure                             Details                                                Examples
 Consensus             Establish formal dispute resolution and            Strengthen security structures, advance economic
 Mechanisms            weighted voting to improve decision-               projects, and promote minilateral cooperation.
                       making efficiency.
 Regional Focus        Realign expansion to prioritise Central            Limiting non-regional memberships post-Belarus,
                       Asian stability over global outreach.              2024, to maintain focus.
 Economic              Promote an SCO Free Trade Area and                 SCO Agreement on Creating Favourable Conditions for
 Integration           Development Bank to boost trade.                   International Road Transportation.
 Counter-              Strengthen RATS with binding                       Sahand-Antiterror-2025 drill for enhanced regional
 Terrorism             commitments to combat terrorism                    cooperation through tactical intelligence sharing, joint
                       effectively.                                       pursuits.
 Cultural Ties         SCO should deepen cultural ties through            Showcasing of Yoga, Ayurveda, and traditional arts
                       people-to-people initiatives to build trust.       in SCO member states to strengthen cultural
                                                                          understanding and soft power engagement.
 Promote Local         Implement the roadmap for gradually                Over 97% of trade between Russia and other SCO
 Currencies            increasing the share of national currencies        partners currently uses national currencies.
                       in mutual settlements.
 Respectful            SCO should prioritise sovereign, transparent       Promoting the INSTC instead of the BRI.
 Connectivity          connectivity over unsustainable projects.
 Projects
The SCO is a vital platform for India to engage with Central Asia, counter terrorism, and enhance connectivity, despite challenges
such as China’s dominance. India’s balanced diplomacy strengthens the SCO’s development focus, fostering regional stability. By
promoting consensus and cooperation, SCO can reshape global multilateralism for a multipolar world.

5             BIMSTEC

The Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation (BIMSTEC), established in 1997, is a
seven-member grouping - Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Thailand - linking South and Southeast Asia.
It promotes economic growth, security, and connectivity and has emerged as a practical alternative to SAARC, aligning with India’s
Act East and Neighbourhood First policies to enhance its Indo-Pacific role.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.     Do you think BIMSTEC is a parallel organization like SAARC? What are the similarities
            and dissimilarities between the two? How Indian foreign policy objectives realized by
            forming a new organization? (2022)

Evolution and Structure
      Aspect                                                           Details
 Genesis                Initiated in 1997 through the Bangkok Declaration as BIST-EC (Bangladesh, India, Sri Lanka,
                        Thailand). Myanmar later joined in 1997, as did Nepal & Bhutan in 2004, forming BIMSTEC.
 Membership             Seven nations: Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Thailand, representing 22%
                        of the global population and a combined GDP of $4.5 trillion.
 Structure              Governed by Summits, Ministerial Meetings, and Senior Officials’ Meetings, with a permanent
                        Secretariat in Dhaka, Bangladesh, since 2014. Each member leads one of seven priority pillars.

                   sustainably, aligning with SAGAR & MAHASAGAR               (BIMReN)

and Southeast Asia. By advancing the Bangkok Vision 2030 and initiatives like BODHI and DPI, BIMSTEC can foster a
resilient, prosperous Indo-Pacific aligned with India's strategic interests.


        6              QUAD

The QUAD is a strategic partnership of India, the United States, Japan, and Australia that promotes a free, open, and inclusive
Indo-Pacific. Evolving from the 2004 tsunami cooperation and revived in 2017, it focuses on maritime security, resilient supply
chains, critical technologies, climate action, and disaster response.

beginning                    recognised their joint naval capacity.

                                                                                   in 2021

                                telecom                                            Technology Working Group

                                                                                   QUAD partners

                                                                                   2024

 Aspects of ‘Strengthening the QUAD Act’
The Strengthening the Quad Act, passed by the U.S. House of Representatives in February 2024, directs the U.S. State Department
to develop a strategy enhancing cooperation with the QUAD.


Way Forward
      Strategic Pillar                                                    Description
 Institutionalisation            Move from informal dialogue to structured coordination through regular summits, ministerial
 of QUAD                         meetings, and working groups.
 Economic & Trade                Transforming from a security forum into a strategic trade bloc to counter economic coercion
 Security                        and secure vital supply chains.
 Maritime Domain                 Expanding real-time tracking capabilities to cover larger portions of the Indian Ocean,
 Awareness                       specifically focusing on dark shipping.
 Critical                        Setting global standards for democratic tech and securing semiconductor and 5G/6G
 Technology                      infrastructures.
 Infrastructure &                Provide transparent, sustainable alternatives to debt-heavy infrastructure models.
 Connectivity
 Regional Outreach               Engaging with partners like South Korea or ASEAN states through functional, issue-based
                                 cooperation without formal membership.
 Development & Security          QUAD must maintain its image as a public-goods provider rather than a military grouping.
 Balance
The Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD) has evolved from a security dialogue into a practical partnership delivering
security, technology, connectivity, and public goods in the Indo-Pacific. It reflects a shift from military alignment to collective
resilience and rule-based cooperation. As often described, QUAD is less an alliance and more an “anchor of stability” in an
uncertain Indo-Pacific - a balancing power without a formal bloc.
pyq: Do you think BIMSTEC is a parallel organization like SAARC? What are the similarities and dissimilarities between the two? How Indian foreign policy objectives realized by forming a new organization? (2022)

chapters.t: AUKUS: Strategic Partnership for the Indo-Pacific
chapters.text:
7             AUKUS: Strategic Partnership
                      for the Indo-Pacific
AUKUS is a trilateral strategic security partnership between Australia, the United Kingdom, and the United States,
launched in 2021 to strengthen deterrence, defence technology, and strategic stability in the Indo-Pacific. It reflects the
growing emphasis on minilateralism, advanced capability-sharing, and rules-based regional order amid intensifying
geopolitical competition.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    The newly tri-nation partnership AUKUS is aimed at countering China’s ambitions in
            the Indo-Pacific region. Is it going to supersede the existing partnership in the region?
            Discuss the strength and impact of AUKUS in the present scenario. (2021)


Significance and Role of AUKUS in the Indo-Pacific
     Aspect                        Role and Significance                                          Example
 Strategic          Shifts the naval balance of power in the Indo-Pacific by    Rotational basing of US and UK submarines in
 Deterrence         introducing high-endurance SSNs into the South China        Australia began in 2027 to build operational
                    Sea.                                                        readiness
 Technological      Fast-tracks the military application of AI and              The ADM (Australian Defence Magazine)
 Edge               autonomous systems to counter numerical advantages.         AUKUS Pillar II Summit focused on
                                                                                integrating AI into maritime surveillance.
 Regional           Pulling middle powers (Japan, South Korea, Philippines)     Japan began formal consultations for Pillar 2
 Magnet             toward a Western-aligned security architecture.             (Advanced Tech) cooperation in early 2026
 Security           Supplements existing frameworks like the QUAD by            The Maritime Big Play series demonstrated
 Architecture       handling the "hard security"/military dimension             trilateral autonomous undersea drone
                                                                                coordination.

Importance and Benefits for India
      Aspect                            Description                                             Benefit
 Countering            A stronger Australian Navy keeps the PLAN           Reduces the immediate naval pressure on India in
 China                 (Chinese Navy) occupied in the Pacific              the Indian Ocean Region (IOR).

 Diversification       Pacific, forcing France to find a new defence       (after the AUKUS snub) offers India better
                       partner in the Indo-Pacific to remain relevant.     leverage.
 Technological         Potential for "Pillar 2" expansion to like-minded   Recent discussions suggest India could benefit from
 Spillover             partners like India in the future.                  AUKUS-led standards in quantum & cybersecurity.
 Net Security          AUKUS handles high-end Pacific deterrence,          India remains the Net Security Provider in its
                       allowing India to focus on the IOR.                 primary area of interest.


AUKUS and Existing Partnerships
  Aspect                          Comparison                                             Strategic Outcome
AUKUS vs.       AUKUS handles Hard Security (warfighting), while           AUKUS does not supersede QUAD; it specializes
QUAD            QUAD pivots to Soft Security (diplomacy/public             the security architecture, allowing QUAD to remain
                goods).                                                    non-military.
AUKUS vs.       Challenges ASEAN Centrality by creating an elite,          Shifts the regional center of gravity from Southeast
ASEAN           external security core.                                    Asian consensus to Great Power deterrence.
AUKUS vs.       Moves beyond intelligence sharing (Five Eyes) into         Evolves a trusted circle into a unified combat
Five Eyes       deep industrial and military integration.                  industrial base.

AUKUS: Countering China’s Ambitions
  Strategic Goal                         Description                                             Example
Neutralizing Area       Breaking China’s Anti-Access / Area Denial           SSN-AUKUS submarines can operate inside
Denial                  (A2/AD) capabilities in the South China Sea.         Chinese sensor bubbles without being detected.
Multi-Front             Forcing China to secure its Southern Flank           The Submarine Rotational Force-West (SRF-W)
Deterrence              (Australia) as well as its Eastern Flank (Japan/     in Perth ensures a 24/7 allied presence in the
                        Taiwan).                                             Indian Ocean.
Combatting Grey         Using Pillar 2 of AI and undersea drones to          Deployment of underwater autonomous vehicles
Zone Tactics            monitor Chinese maritime militia and research        (UAVs) to track Chinese dual-use vessels in
                        vessels.                                             real-time.
Securing Taiwan         Deterring a blockade or invasion by making           AUKUS Pillar 2 has fast-tracked thousands of
                        the cost of military action unacceptably high.       attritable autonomous drones (sea and air)
                                                                             intended to swarm any invading fleet in the
                                                                             Taiwan Strait.

Issues and Concerns with AUKUS
     Aspect                        Description                                              Example
Nuclear               Use of Highly Enriched Uranium sets a        Critics argue it exploits a "loophole" in the Non-
Proliferation         precedent for non-nuclear states using       Proliferation Treaty (NPT)
                      nuclear fuel.
Regional              The introduction of high-end nuclear         While the Philippines supports AUKUS, Indonesia and
Instability           tech into a volatile region risks creating   Malaysia fear permanent allied presence in the Malacca
                      security dilemmas for neutral                Strait threatens the ZOPFAN (Zone of Peace, Freedom,
                      neighbours.                                  and Neutrality) status.
Operational and       The logistical hurdle of building            Australia’s limited nuclear infrastructure and the US
logistical            Nuclear-Ready facilities (berths,            -UK’ shipyards are already struggling to meet their own
challenges            high-purity water, and waste storage).       domestic production targets.
Diplomatic            AUKUS has sidelined many traditional         The EU expressed dissatisfaction with Australia's decision,
Friction              allies from the Western World.               and bilateral Australia-EU free trade talks were initially
                                                                   postponed.
US Regulatory         Strict export controls (ITAR) hinder the     US bureaucratic red tape remains a major bottleneck for
Hurdles               very technology sharing AUKUS                the UK and Australia as of 2026.
                      promises.
Arms Race             AUKUS acts as a catalyst for rapid           North Korea conducted trials of its nuclear-armed
                      naval expansion among regional               underwater drone (Haeil-4) in response to AUKUS sub
                      competitors.                                 rotations.

Concerns for India
     Aspect                            Description                                              Example
Strategic             Fear that AUKUS may lead to a bloc-based              India prefers a multipolar world order rather than a
Autonomy              security order, forcing India to choose sides.        US-led security architecture.


 Overshadowing           There is a concern that the hard-security mandate      If AUKUS becomes the primary security pillar,
 QUAD                    of AUKUS may overshadow the QUAD.                      India's role in the QUAD's security logic may dilute.
 Technological           While AUKUS shares top-tier tech (AI,                  India has to pay higher costs or face stricter
 Exclusion               Quantum) among its three members, India                export controls for the same underwater drone
                         remains outside this "inner circle" of elite           tech that AUKUS members share freely.
                         technology sharing.
 Crowding the            Increased presence of extra-regional powers            While helpful against China, it complicates India's
 IOR                     (US/UK) in the Indian Ocean.                           role as the "Net Security Provider" in the IOR.

Way Forward
          Aspect                                                             Description
 Mutual balance                India should ensure that AUKUS and QUAD collaborate seamlessly to maintain regional
                               stability.
 Submarine Diplomacy           Capitalize on the strategic shift in France-AUKUS relations to accelerate India’s indigenous
                               submarine programs, specifically Project-75I and SSNs.
 Pillar 2 Engagement           Seek functional cooperation in Pillar 2 (AI, Cyber) without joining the formal military alliance.
 ASEAN Centrality              Work with AUKUS partners to assuage the fears of SE Asian nations regarding regional
                               stability.
 Nuclear Non-                  Advocate robust IAEA safeguards, transparency, and adherence to global non-proliferation
 Proliferation                 norms to ensure that nuclear submarine cooperation under AUKUS does not weaken the
                               international non-proliferation regime.
AUKUS represents a paradigm shift in Indo-Pacific security, moving from diplomatic consultation to high-end military
integration. For India, the challenge lies in balancing its strategic autonomy while leveraging the China-deterring benefits that
this trilateral pact provides.
