8              India-Middle East-Europe
                       Economic Corridor (IMEC)
The India–Middle East–Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC), launched at the 2023 G20 New Delhi Summit, is a multimodal
connectivity project. It links India, the Arabian Gulf, and Europe. Envisioned as a modern-day Spice Route, it integrates ports,
railways, roads, and sea routes. It also includes high-speed data cables and green hydrogen pipelines. The corridor aims to boost
trade, energy flows, and digital connectivity across regions.


Significance of IMEC (India & Global Perspective)
         Aspect                           Description                                           Examples
 Enhanced                    Connects India to Europe via a shorter        IMEC route linking Mumbai → UAE (Jebel Ali) →
 Connectivity and            multimodal route, bypassing chokepoints       Saudi rail → Haifa (Israel) → Europe
 Supply Chains               like the Suez Canal and Bab-el-Mandeb.
 Trade & Economic            Connects India’s largest trading blocs        Expected 30% reduction in logistics cost and 40%
 Expansion                   (EU & GCC), boosting trade volumes.           reduction in transit time vs the Suez Canal route.
 Energy Security             Aligns with India's National Green            Planned green hydrogen pipelines and hubs in
 & Exports                   Hydrogen Mission and Europe's RE              UAE & Saudi Arabia supplying Europe
                             Power EU goals.
 Digital Integration         Deployment of high-speed undersea data        Enhances digital trade, fintech interoperability, and
                             cables across the three regions.              secure data flow.
 Global Power                Enhances India’s geopolitical stature and     India’s leadership in G20 2023 launch of IMEC
 Aspiration                  integration into global value chains.         alongside US & EU
 Investment                  Encourages foreign investments via            Participation of G7 countries under PGII funding
 Attraction                  improved connectivity and economic            infrastructure projects.
                             integration.
 Deepening                   Strengthens ties with key partners like the   Strategic cooperation among India–USA–EU–
 Multilateral Ties           USA & EU and deepens India's strategic        Saudi–UAE partners in IMEC
                             footprint in the Arabian Peninsula beyond
                             energy imports (Act West Policy).


 Post-Oil Economy            Supports Gulf countries’ diversification        Saudi Vision 2030 and UAE diversification into
 (Global)                    beyond oil.                                     logistics and digital economy hubs
 Regional Integration        Promotes economic interdependence,              Potential expansion to Jordan, Egypt, Oman, and
 & Stability                 reducing conflict incentives.                   Turkey through corridor extensions.
 Counter to BRI              Provides a transparent, sustainable             IMEC follows debt sustainability practices to prevent
                             alternative to China’s Belt and Road            debt traps, unlike BRI's strategic asset seizures.
                             Initiative.
 Reducing carbon             The IMEC network will improve                   It offers a faster land-based railway network (up to
 footprint                   efficiency, reduce costs and generate           40% faster) that is significantly less carbon-intensive
                             employment, also helping the                    than ocean freight, reducing fuel consumption per
                             participating geographies to lower their        tonne-mile.
                             carbon footprint.

Challenges Before IMEC
       Conflict                           Description                                                Example
 Middle -East           The Israel-Palestine conflict has stalled the          Security concerns at Haifa Port (Israel) have
 Instability            Northern Corridor (Jordan-Israel-                      increased insurance premiums and deterred
                        Mediterranean).                                        investors.
 Security Risks         Security tensions in West Asia pose risks to the       Houthi attacks in the Red Sea following the Gaza
                        seamless functioning of IMEC by disrupting             conflict disrupted global shipping routes and
                        trade routes, logistics chains, and infrastructure     exposed the vulnerability of IMEC-related trade
                        investments.                                           networks.
 Saudi-Israel Ties      Normalisation of ties, a prerequisite for the          Saudi Arabia insists on a two-state solution before
                        corridor, is currently on hold.                        fully operationalising the rail link to Israel.
 Infrastructure         Missing rail links in Saudi Arabia and Jordan          A global economic slowdown in 2025 has led to a
 Gaps                   require massive capital.                               financing gap for the cross-border segments.
 Geopolitics            Opposition from Turkey (seeking an Iraq-Turkey         Turkey has demanded to be a formal part of the
                        route) and Iran (pushing the INSTC).                   corridor to avoid being bypassed.
 BRI                    China’s BRI is a decade ahead and is larger in         BRI features thousands of projects totalling
                        scale compared to the nascent IMEC.                    approximately $1tn, while IMEC is in early
                                                                               planning stages.

                Current Status (as of April 2026): Amid West Asia's trade disruptions because of the
       USA-Israel vs Iran war, the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) is regaining focus as an
      alternative route. While geopolitical issues hinder its full scope, India and the UAE are strengthening their
     "foundational leg" through digital infrastructure like the Virtual Trade Corridor. This aims to ease trade and
                    maintain the IMEC's vision as an economic opportunity, supported by the US.

Way Forward
        Aspect                                                           Description
 Strengthening             As seen in the Iran vs USA-Israel war, regional cooperation on defence and security, sharing
 regional stability        intelligence, and forging joint initiatives to counter security risks are necessary steps to safeguard the
                           corridor’s successful development and operation.
 Port Modernization        Expedite the Vadhvan Port project to handle the massive container surge expected from IMEC.
 Establishing              Different policies and bureaucracies will result in delays and operational inefficiencies; hence,
 favourable trade          establishing favourable trade and investment regulations among the participating nations is of key
 regulations               importance.
 Diplomatic                Maintain high-level engagement with Egypt to ensure IMEC is seen as a complement to the Suez
 Balancing                 Canal, not a threat, to avoid diplomatic friction.
 MAITRI Portal             Fully operationalise the Digital Logistics Interface to allow paperless transit across Saudi and
                           European borders.

For India, IMEC is the "Modern Silk Road". While regional conflicts in 2026 present short-term hurdles, the corridor is essential
for India to break out of its geographical constraints and integrate into the heart of the global economy

chapters.t: ASEAN-India Relations: The Core of the Indo-Pacific
chapters.text:
9             ASEAN-India Relations: The
                      Core of the Indo-Pacific
India–ASEAN relations, elevated to a Comprehensive Strategic Partnership in 2022, are a cornerstone of India’s Act
East Policy and a key pillar of the Indo-Pacific framework. Since the establishment of dialogue relations in 1992, it
has evolved from sectoral cooperation to a robust engagement covering trade, maritime security, digital infrastructure,
and cultural connectivity.

Significance of India-ASEAN Relations
  Dimension                         Description                                    Strategic/Economic Benefit
 Economic           ASEAN is India's 4th largest trading               Provides a market of 680mn people for Indian pharma,
 Pillar             partner and offers India significant               agritech, and digital services.
                    opportunities to expand its export reach.
 Indo - Pacific     ASEAN sits at the heart of the Indo-Pacific;       ASEAN forms a key pillar of India’s “Act East” policy &
 Centrality         its centrality is vital for regional stability.    Indo-Pacific strategy, highlighting shared interests in region.
 Connectivity       Gateway to the North-East; links India to          Projects like the Trilateral Highway transform India's
                    Pacific supply chains.                             landlocked NE into a land-linked hub.
 Maritime           Promotes a rules-based maritime order to           Designation of 2026 as the ‘ASEAN-India Year of
 Security           ensure safe navigation                             Maritime Cooperation’,
 Cultural/Soft      Shared Buddhist and Hindu heritage                 India’s Buddhist diplomacy strengthens political goodwill
 Power              (Sanskritic links) foster people-to-people ties.   in Thailand, Vietnam, and Cambodia.
 Energy             ASEAN energy resources can help India              India-Vietnam energy projects in the South China Sea via
                    diversify supplies and meet its growing            ONGC Videsh and PetroVietnam since the late 1980s.
                    economic and energy needs.

Areas of Cooperation between India and ASEAN
     Aspect                            Description                                                Examples
 Institutional       Ties evolved from Sectoral Dialogue (1992) →          Annual India–ASEAN Summit
 Engagement          Dialogue Partner (1996) → Summit Level
                     (2002).
 Connectivity        Enhancing physical and economic linkages.             IMT Highway; Kaladan Project
 Defence &           Expanding cooperation in maritime security            ASEAN-India Maritime Exercise, ADMM+,
 Security            and regional stability.                               SAGAR vision
 Socio-Cultural      Promotes people-to-people ties and cultural           Student Exchange Programme, diplomat training,
 Cooperation         exchange.                                             think tank networks, lecture series
 Education &         Strengthening academic and policy research            ASEAN-India Centre at Research and Information
 Research            collaboration.                                        System for Developing Countries
 Dialogue            Platforms for strategic, economic, and cultural       Delhi Dialogue (since 2009) – Track 1.5 forum
 Mechanisms          discussions.
 Financial           India supports ASEAN development through              ASEAN-India Cooperation Fund, S&T Fund,
 Cooperation         dedicated funds.                                      Green Fund

India-ASEAN Relations Challenges
    Challenge                           Description                                               Example
 Trade Deficit         India faces a persistent and widening trade        The trade deficit reached $45 billion in 2024-25,
                       imbalance favouring ASEAN.                         largely due to electronics and palm oil imports.
 Non-Tariff            Indian exports (pharma, meat) face complex         Strict Rules of Origin are often bypassed by Chinese

10                  Group of Seven (G7)

democracies shaping global economic governance, security, and energy policy. Though not a member, India is a regular invitee and
key partner, representing the Global South and collaborating on supply chains, technology, energy security, and geopolitical stability.

11                  North Atlantic Treaty
                         Organization (NATO )
The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), established in 1949 through the Washington Treaty, is a transatlantic
intergovernmental military alliance. It is based on the principle of collective defence, where an attack on one is considered an attack
on all (Article 5). Its objective is to safeguard the freedom and security of its members through political and military means.

12                 Gulf Cooperation Council
                        (GCC) - India Relations
India-GCC relations are a vital, multifaceted partnership based on proximity, strong economic ties, and a large diaspora. As a
cornerstone of India’s “extended neighborhood,” the GCC (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman, and Bahrain) supplies over
60% of India’s energy and hosts nearly 10 million Indians, with bilateral trade exceeding USD 178 billion (FY25). The relationship
has evolved from a buyer-seller model to a strategic partnership in investment, security, and technology in recent years.

​​Evolution of India-GCC Relations (1981–2026)

1              The United Nations (UN)
                       & Global Governance
Established in 1945, the UN is the premier multilateral organization with 193 members. As it approaches its 80th anniversary
(UN80) in 2025–26, it faces a "legitimacy crisis" due to its 20th-century structure.
                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    What are the main functions of the United Nations Economic and Social Council
            (ECOSOC)? Explain different functional commissions attached to it. (2017)
      2.    What are the aims and objectives of the McBride Commission of the UNESCO? What is
            India’s position on these? (2016)

The UN at 80 remains a "Humanitarian Giant but a Political Dwarf," indispensable for global development while paralyzed by
a 1945-era security structure. For its future survival, the UN80 reforms must transition the organization from a "Victors' Club" to
a representative body that integrates the strategic weight of the Global South and rising powers like India.

1.1 Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC)
Established in 1945 as a principal UN organ, ECOSOC serves as the central platform for coordinating the economic, social, and
environmental work of 15 specialized agencies and various regional commissions. Its primary objective is to advance the three
dimensions of sustainable development - economic, social, and environmental - while fostering global dialogue and policy consensus
to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

1.2 Aims and Objectives of the UNESCO McBride Commission
The McBride Commission (1977–1980), led by Seán MacBride, examined global communication imbalances, aiming to democratise
communication, reduce media concentration, bridge the North–South divide, and promote a New World Information and Communication
Order (NWICO).

 International Cooperation          Encourage technical and cultural collaboration for peace

 Coherence                  coordinate the IMF and World Bank.

 Operations                 peacebuilding initiatives.
The United Nations continues to be the cornerstone of global governance, fostering peace, development, and international cooperation.
To remain effective in an evolving world, it must embrace reforms that make it more representative, accountable, and responsive to
contemporary challenges

chapters.part: Important International Institutions, Agencies and Fora - Their Structure, Mandate
chapters.text:
2            United Nations Security Council
                       (UNSC)
The United Nations Security Council (UNSC) is the principal organ of the United Nations responsible for maintaining international
peace and security. Established in 1945, it has 15 members — five permanent members with veto power and ten non-permanent
members elected for two-year terms. The UNSC adopts binding resolutions, authorises peacekeeping missions, imposes sanctions,
and responds to global conflicts and security threats.
                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.      ‘Terrorism has become a significant threat to global peace and security.’ Evaluate the
             effectiveness of the United Nations Security Council's Counter Terrorism Committee (CTC)
             and its associated bodies in addressing and mitigating this threat at the international
             level. (2024)
     2.      Discuss the impediments India is facing in its pursuit of a permanent seat in UN Security
             Council. (2015)

Role of the UNSC
         Function                                                             Examples
 Peaceful                    Mediation efforts in the Sudan Civil War (2025-26) and the facilitation of maritime border talks in
 Settlement                  the Eastern Mediterranean.
 Enforcement                 Implementation of Resolution 2803, which endorsed a ceasefire and aid delivery plan for Gaza;
 Action                      Sanctions on gangs in Haiti.
 Peacekeeping                Mandates for UNMISS (South Sudan) and MINUSCA (Central African Republic) to protect
                             civilians from ethnic violence.
 Counter-                    The 2026 Delhi Declaration implementation - tracking the use of Unmanned Aerial Systems
 Terrorism                   (Drones) and crypto-terror financing.

Relevance of UNSC
      Aspect                       Description                                                 Example
 Global High           The only forum where all nuclear            The Council remains the only place where the US and China
 Table                 powers (P5) must negotiate.                 coordinate on AI in Nuclear Command & Control.
 Sanctions             Unmatched ability to freeze assets          The addition of Sami al-Ajuz (Hajji Hamid) to the ISIL/
 Power                 and restrict travel of global spoilers.     Al-Qaida list.
 Legal                 Grants international legitimacy to the      Nations still seek UNSC Blue Helmets for Somalia because
 Seal                  use of force.                               regional forces lack the same legal standing and funding.

The Need for Reform
         Aspect                            Description                                               Example
 Veto Paralysis           P5 members use the veto to protect their own         Security Council divisions and vetoes during the Gaza
                          interests or those of their allies.                  conflict prevented the adoption of several ceasefire
                                                                               resolutions, highlighting the need for veto reform
 Representational         The Council reflects the 1945 World Order. Its       Africa (54 nations) and India have zero permanent
 Deficit                  Eurocentric approach ignores Global South.           seats, leading to a Crisis of Legitimacy.
 Working                  Lack of transparency; Closed - door consultations    Historically, only the US, the UK, and France draft
 Methods                  exclude the E10 (elected members).                   most resolutions, sidelining emerging powers.
 Financial /              Heavy reliance on a few donors (USA/                 The UN80 Initiative aims to streamline agencies.
 Structural               China) creates budgetary blackmail risks.


Reasons Strengthening India’s Claims for UNSC Seat
     Aspect                                  Description                                                Example
Demographic              India, with 1/6th of the world's humanity, has more          India is the largest demographic stakeholder
Weight                   ground for inclusion in the UNSC.                            in global peace.
Economic                 India is a key engine of global growth and has               India is officially the 4th largest economy
Powerhouse               transitioned from a balancing to a leading power.            (nominal) and 3rd largest (PPP).
Peacekeeping             Historically and currently, one of the top contributors      India led the 2025-26 Global South Women
Leader                   of troops, police, and specialized personnel.                Peacekeepers Conference.
Global South             India has emerged as the institutional architect for         India hosted the 3rd Voice of Global South
Voice                    developing nations, bridging the North-South divide.         Summit and secured permanent G20
                                                                                      membership for the AU.
Nuclear                  A recognized nuclear weapon state with a no-first-           Full member of MTCR, Wassenaar
Responsibility           use policy and non-proliferation credentials.                Arrangement, and Australia Group, proving
                                                                                      its rule-maker status.

Challenges to India's permanent membership
   Aspect                           Description                                                   Example
The China          China has historically used technical holds and        In recent strategic dialogues, China has softened its tone
Factor             linked India's bid to Japan’s bid (which it            to respecting India's aspirations but has yet to provide a
                   strongly opposes).                                     written endorsement.
The G4             India is tied to the G4 (India, Japan, Brazil,         India is currently exploring internal differentiation within
Package            Germany). If one of them is blocked, the               the G4 to see if a staggered entry is possible in the
                   entire group stalls.                                   2026-2030 cycle.
Strategic          India's independent stance on global conflicts         Critics point to India's abstentions on key resolutions (like
Autonomy           leads to reliability concerns among some               Ukraine) as a sign that it may not always align with
                   Western P5 members.                                    current P5 security priorities.

Successes of the UNSC Counter-Terrorism Committee (CTC)
      Aspect                              Description                                               Examples
Financial Choking         Freezes assets and restricts travel of listed       The addition of Hajji Hamid to the 1267 list.
                          terrorists in coordination with the FATF.
Emerging Tech             Sets norms on terror use of new                     Delhi Declaration (2022–26) on drones &
Leadership                technologies.                                       encryption.
Institutional             The CTED (Executive Directorate)                    Tajikistan Visit: CTED experts conducted a follow-
Auditing                  performs onsite inspections to identify and         up audit to modernise Aviation Security and Border
                          fix national security loopholes.                    Management

Limitations of the UNSC Counter-Terrorism Committee (CTC)
 Aspect                       Description                                                    Examples
Definition       The absence of a legal definition of           CCIT Delay: India’s Comprehensive Convention on International
Deadlock         "terrorism" allows states to classify          Terrorism remains stuck in 2026 due to lack of consensus on
                 political dissidents as terrorists             state-sponsored actors.
Veto &           P5 members use technical holds to              China used a hold to designate a high-ranking commander in the
Political        protect strategic assets or proxy groups       Lashkar-e-Taiba network as a terrorist.
Bias             from sanctions.
Human            CTC-mandated surveillance and data             The UN Special Rapporteur issued a report on the misuse of
Rights           laws are often misused by authoritarian        Biometric Counter-Terror Databases for domestic mass
Concerns         regimes to suppress dissent.                   surveillance.


Way Forward
    Component                             Description                                         Strategic Goal
 Expansion Size          Increase the Council from 15 to 25 or 26             To balance efficiency with broader regional
                         members.                                             representation.
 New Permanent           Expand permanent seats for equal representation      May have 2 for Africa, 2 for Asia-Pacific
 Seats                   by updating the UN rules to include modern           (including India) and 1 for Latin America.
                         leaders like India.
 Veto Flexibility        New members would not exercise the veto for a        Address P5's worry about too many vetoes so
                         10-to-15 year review period.                         they agree to a faster entry for new members.
 Democratic              Focus on contribution to peace and demographic       To make India’s population an undeniable factor
 Merit                   representation.                                      for legitimacy.
The United Nations Security Council remains the central pillar of the United Nations global security architecture, tasked with
preserving international peace and stability. However, evolving geopolitical realities and demands for equitable representation
highlight the urgent need for institutional reforms. A more representative and effective UNSC is essential to address contemporary
security challenges and strengthen multilateral governance in the 21st century.


India and Global
   Geopolitics

chapters.text:
1              India and Emerging
                       World Order
In the evolving landscape, India has transitioned from being a balancing power to a Sovereign Pole in a fragmented, multipolar
world. As the chair of BRICS and a permanent invitee to the G7, India is the only major actor effectively straddling the divide
between the West (Global North) and the rising East/South.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    “The long-sustained image of India as a leader of the oppressed and marginalised
            Nations has disappeared on account of its new found role in the emerging global order.”
            Elaborate. (2019)

                         interest, avoiding bloc-politics.            inducting U.S. MQ-9B drones.

                         (G7 vs. BRICS) to maximize diplomatic        summit in New Delhi while remaining a major defense
                         and economic leverage.                       partner of the U.S.
 Global Friend           Positioning India as a reliable responder    Operation Sindhu: India leading the evacuation of 15+
                         & a bridge between the global North and      nationalities from Middle East conflict zones.
                         South.

 De-risking              supply chains (specifically China).          high-tech imports and textile exports.

                         good to challenge the monopoly of global     France, UAE, and Singapore) now accept UPI for
                         big-tech platforms.                          cross-border settlements.

                              governance.                                   Artemis.

                              power gaps.                                   region.

                              power.                                        semiconductor export controls.
 Climate Politics             Shift to climate finance and compensation     Loss & Damage Fund tensions.
                              disputes

Strategic Opportunities for India
        Area                                Description                                           Example

                          global upstream - downstream data sovereign.           replacing GPS for their needs.

                          renewable energy                                       for export to EU

                          mining.

                          universities as cost-effective.                        Zanzibar
Indo-Pacific Bridge       Leverage the central position to connect the           Joint Patrols in Malacca Strait.
                          Mediterranean with the South China Sea.

India’s Strategic Contributions to Global Peace
      Aspect                         Description                                            Example
UN Peacekeeping         Providing specialised, high-skill units to    India deployed an AI-integrated UAV surveillance
(PKO)                   stabilise high-risk conflict zones under      unit to MONUSCO (DRC) to protect civilians from
                        the UN flag.                                  rebels.
HADR                    Rapid deployment of military and              Deployment of C-17 Globemasters with portable
(First Responder)       medical assets to mitigate the fallout of     oxygen plants & field hospitals after the Central
                        natural disasters.                            Asian seismic crisis.
Maritime Security       Acting as a security bridge in contested      India as a net security provider in the Strait of
                        chokepoints to ensure the flow of             Malacca
                        essential goods.
Cyber- Peacekeeping     Leading the global effort to protect          India hosted its 1st International Subsea Cable
                        digital public goods from state-              Systems Conference in New Delhi in March 2025
                        sponsored hacking.
Mediation               Using non-aligned status to host quiet        India hosted backchannel discussions on the Russia–
(Track 1.5)             diplomacy sessions between conflicting        Ukraine humanitarian corridor issue.
                        parties.

Challenges for India in a Multipolar World Order
      Aspect                             Description                                             Example

                        blocs while maintaining strategic autonomy.           Russia amid the Ukraine conflict.
China’s                 Military, economic and technological rise of          Border tensions along LAC and China’s BRI
Assertiveness           China challenges India’s regional and global          expansion in South Asia
                        influence.
Fragmented Global       Weakening multilateral institutions reduces           WTO dispute settlement paralysis.
Governance              predictable rule-based engagement.
Technology              Competition over semiconductors, AI, cyber            Restrictions on advanced chip access and tech
Dependence              and critical technologies.                            supply chains.
Regional Instability    Conflicts in neighbourhood and extended               Afghanistan instability and West Asia tensions.
                        neighbourhood affect security.
Energy & Resource       Growing rivalry over critical minerals and            Lithium and rare earth supply competition.
Competition             energy security.


Way Forward
           Aspect                                                         Description
 Strategic Autonomy             India needs to maintain its strategic autonomy through multi-alignment, balancing the US,
                                Russia, and China
 Institutional Reform           G4 & L.69 Consolidation: Leading a text-based negotiation for UNSC permanent membership
                                during the 2026-27 non-permanent term.

                                under BIMSTEC.

                                critical tech sovereignty by 2030.

                                tracking for all 22 IORA member nations.
 Domestic Strength              Boost manufacturing, defence, and digital capacity to improve its credibility and power
                                dynamics.
 Diplomacy                      Expand Indo-Pacific, ASEAN, and African partnerships
 Soft Power                     Promote culture, aid, and AI/data governance
 Global South                   Champion energy, climate, and food security issues in this region
India’s rise as a sovereign pole will hinge on balancing strategic autonomy with active global engagement. By strengthening domestic
capacity, deepening multi-alignment, and leading on technology, climate, and Global South issues, India can shape an inclusive
multipolar order. Its ability to bridge rival blocs and deliver global public goods will define its future influence.

chapters.text:
2                India and Global South
India has emerged as a leading voice of the Global South, advocating equitable development, climate justice, and reform of global
governance institutions. Through platforms like the G20 Presidency, development partnerships, and South-South cooperation, India
positions itself as a bridge between developed and developing worlds.

Significance of the Global South for India
        Aspect                          Significance                                              Example

                                                                           advice.

                           Chinese models.                                 prioritize Global South challenges.

                           inclusion.                                      Mauritius, Sierra Leone, and Trinidad and Tobago

         Aspect                              Significance                                             Example

 Sovereign                   components.                                          design lab

    Aspect                        Description                                                  Example

 Scale              China’s mega projects.                            vs. India’s Kaladan Multi-Modal Transit Project.

Way forward
       Aspect                                                           Description

 Reform                  provide fairer risk assessments for developing nation bonds.

 Leadership              Global South shipping fleets.
India’s role in the Global South ultimately lies in transforming solidarity into solutions, moving beyond representation toward
delivering finance, technology, and development outcomes. By championing reformed multilateralism, resilient supply chains,
and human-centric globalization, India seeks to reshape global governance to be more democratic and development-oriented.

3            Rise of Minilaterals
The "Rise of Minilaterals" (also known as "Small Tables Diplomacy") has become the defining feature of India’s foreign policy. As
traditional multilateral bodies like the UN and WTO face paralysis, these smaller, purpose-driven groups offer a faster, more flexible
way to solve specific problems.

        Aspect                               Description                                              Example

 and Green Growth           technologies required for its clean energy            energy project in Gujarat under the I2U2
                            transition.                                           framework.

 Resilience                 supply chains.                                        technology, and UAE logistics.
 Enhanced Global            Participation in a high-profile grouping with         India contributes to shaping agendas on food
 Influence                  major economic and technological powers               security, infrastructure, and sustainable
                            increases India’s diplomatic weight.                  development within I2U2 initiatives.

                                                                                  independent relations with other major powers.

                            initiatives, positioning India as a central node      Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor can

Way forward
        Aspect                          Description                                              Example

                           structures.                                   I2U2 with a permanent HQ for continuity.

India's foreign policy is the primary multiplier for Atmanirbhar Bharat. India is no longer a swing state but the system integrator of
a multipolar world. By solving domestic challenges with scalable tech and exporting those solutions via agile minilaterals, India has
ensured that the path to global stability now runs through New Delhi.

chapters.text:
4              Space Diplomacy
Space diplomacy is the strategic use of space exploration, technology sharing, and international collaboration to further a nation's
foreign policy objectives and national interests. In today’s geopolitical landscape, it has evolved from a tool of prestige to a core
pillar of Strategic Autonomy.

Significance of Space Diplomacy for India
       Aspect                               Explanation                                               Example
 Soft Power &             Positioning India as the leader of the global south      Expansion of ground stations in Maldives (2026)

 Autonomy                 systems for critical infrastructure and navigation.      achieve assured regional coverage for drones.

 Parity                   rules like lunar mining and orbital traffic.             Governance Forum as a lead partner.

 Security                 surveillance and maritime domain awareness.              in late 2025

 Growth                   startups and the commercial arm (NSIL).                  awareness (SSA) data with Quad partners.

India's Space Diplomacy
    Aspect                             Description                                                   Example

 Leadership        (DPI) to anchor the neighbourhood-first policy.               5G-ready ground station in Bhutan.

 South             counter regional technological hegemony.                      nations (including Vietnam) for the FASAL
 Outreach                                                                        (Forecasting Agricultural output).

 Diplomacy         frontier to prevent space colonialism.                        2026 Lunar Governance Forum.

Critical Challenges in India's Space Diplomacy
   Challenges                       Description                                                 Example
 Reliability          The erosion of India’s status as a             PSLV-C62 failure resulting in the destruction of the strategic
 Deficit              dependable, low-cost launcher due to           EOS-N1 military satellite.
                      successive mission failures.

 Atrophy              positioning system, leading to continued       required for full functioning); the NVS-02 failure delayed
                      reliance on foreign GPS.                       critical replenishment.

India’s space diplomacy is no longer about flag-planting prestige; it is about building the infrastructure of trust. By integrating the
Global South into our data networks and leading the call for a debris-free orbit, India has transitioned from a regional player to a
global anchor of orbital stability.

5               Energy Diplomacy
Energy diplomacy is the strategic use of foreign policy, economic engagement, and international cooperation by states to secure
uninterrupted access to energy resources, stabilize markets, and shape global power relations. In an era where oil routes, gas pipelines,
and renewable technologies influence geopolitics as much as military strength, energy has become a decisive tool of national security
and global influence.

India’s energy diplomacy must transition from energy security to energy leadership, emphasising system resilience, critical
mineral security, and value-chain integration. By leveraging platforms like the International Solar Alliance and Global Biofuels
Alliance, India can strengthen its strategic autonomy, drive green growth, and emerge as a global norm-setter in the clean energy
transition.

6             Indian Diaspora
The Indian Diaspora includes NRIs (Non-Resident Indians), PIOs (Persons of Indian Origin), and OCIs (Overseas Citizens of India).
It is the world’s largest diaspora. It serves as a living bridge for India’s soft power, a source of brain gain, and a critical stabiliser
for India's Forex Reserves and Current Account Deficit (CAD).

The Indian diaspora has emerged as a powerful bridge between India and the world, strengthening economic partnerships, cultural
diplomacy, and strategic influence across regions. Beyond remittances, it contributes to innovation, global leadership networks, and
India’s soft power projection to achieve a connected and cooperative international order.
